items.title: Charlie Chan at Treasure Island released
items.subtitle: Cultural Event • Thu, Aug 31, 1939
items.searchText:
Charlie Chan at Treasure Island released Cultural Event Via Wikipedia:

Charlie and Jimmy Chan are traveling by plane to San Francisco. Jimmy befriends insurance executive Thomas Gregory. Charlie's friend, novelist Paul Essex, dies aboard the aircraft after receiving a radiogram warning him not to ignore "Zodiac". His briefcase mysteriously disappears. Charlie meets with Deputy Police Chief J.J. Kilvaine, and runs into reporter and old friend Peter Lewis. Charlie also meets noted local magician Fred Rhadini, and discusses Essex's death with the three men. Rhadini tells Charlie about Dr. Zodiac, a psychic preying on the rich in San Francisco. Charlie, Rhadini, and Lewis go to Dr. Zodiac's home, where Dr. Zodiac conducts an eerie séance. Lewis' fiancée, Eve Cairo, has been meeting with Dr. Zodiac, angering Lewis. Later, Kilvaine reveals that Essex was poisoned, but can't rule out suicide. Jimmy spends the afternoon following Thomas Gregory, whom he believes stole Essex's briefcase when leaving the plane. He discovers Essex's manuscript in Gregory's hotel room.

That night, Charlie attends Rhadini's magic show at the Golden Gate International Exposition on Treasure Island. Rhadini's clumsy, comic acquaintance, Elmer Kelner, is helping to serve food and drink at the club. Charlie meets Eve Cairo and socialite Bessie Sibley, as well as Rhadini's jealous wife, Myra. During her telepathy act with Fred Rhadini, Eve comes into contact with someone thinking about murder and Charlie is almost killed when a knife is thrown at him.

After the show, Charlie, followed by Rhadini and Lewis, break into Dr. Zodiac's home. They find Jimmy already there. Charlie discovers evidence that Zodiac is a fraud. When Zodiac's Turkish houseman, Abdul, arrives, Charlie searches him and finds the holster that fits the knife. Abdul escapes, and the burglars discover Zodiac's vast files which he uses to frighten and blackmail others. Charlie realizes Bessie Sibley is providing information on others to protect herself, and that Zodiac was blackmailing Essex. Charlie burns Zodiac's office to protect the innocent.

Essex's manuscript is a fictional account of Dr. Zodiac's blackmail scheme, and the next morning Charlie finds that the last page revealing who the murder was committed is missing. Charlie meets with Gregory, who says he is an insurance company detective investigating mysterious suicides. Charlie believes Gregory's claim is false, and Gregory fails to steal the manuscript back. Charlie believes Dr. Zodiac suffers from pseudologia fantastica, and Rhadini challenges Dr. Zodiac to a public test of psychic skills. Dr. Zodiac accepts the claim by leaving a note on the front door of the Temple of Magic where Rhadini performs. It's written on the back side of the missing manuscript page. The manuscript mentions a pygmy arrow; a similar arrow from a display in the foyer of the Temple of Magic is missing.

That night, Charlie, Jimmy, Bessie Sibley, Myra Rhadini, and Peter Lewis attend Rhadini's magic show, where he is assisted by Eve Cairo and Elmer Kelner. Dr. Zodiac appears during the show, and is invited on stage. As Rhadini performs a levitation trick, Zodiac is killed with the pygmy arrow. Dr. Zodiac is revealed to be Abdul. Although a bow is found, it is too brittle to have fired the arrow. Zodiac must have been stabbed with the arrow. Gregory gives Rhadini an alibi after discovering Rhadini's wand in the aisle by his seat. Kilvaine reveals that Gregory is Stewart Salsbury, and that he really is an insurance company executive.

At Kilvaine's suggestion, the murder is re-enacted with Lewis standing in for Zodiac. The secret of Rhadini's levitation trick is revealed, and Rhadini is stabbed in the aisle during the act. Myra uses the "sphinx"—an upright metal pseudo-Egyptian coffin with a hidden elevator in its floor—to go from the stage to the below-stage area, where her husband's dressing room is located. Charlie encourages Eve to try to tap into the mind of the killer. Eve reads Charlie's thoughts, which describe the motivations of Stella Essex, Bessie Sibley, Thomas Gregory, Peter Lewis, Fred Rhadini, and Myra Rhadinia (although without mentioning their names). The mind of Dr. Zodiac interferes with Eve's mind. Eve reads Zodiac's mind, and discovers that the real Dr. Zodiac killed Abdul because only Abdul knew Dr. Zodiac's real identity.

The killer attempts to shoot Eve while she is on stage, but Jimmy spots the pistol and pushes the gun away just in time. Dr. Zodiac is revealed to be Fred Rhadini. While all eyes were on Eve, he sneaked into the wings, ran below the stage, and used the elevator in the sphinx to re-emerge on stage and attempt to kill Eve. Charlie reveals that Rhadini used a wand with a spring trigger to fire the arrow that killed Abdul. He then stabbed himself to divert attention from himself as a suspect.  United States of America

items.searchText:
Michael Mageau is born Birthday Stephen and his twin brother Michael Renault Mageau were born on October 29, 1949. They lived together until their parents divorced and then Stephen moved to Los Angeles to live with their mother, Carmen, while Michael moved to Vallejo with their father, Robert. Michael had problems at school and was arrested in September 1968 for petty theft from a store on Springs Road.

Butterfield, Michael. The Zodiac Killer: The Story of America's Most Elusive Murderer (p. 22). (Function). Kindle Edition. Michael Renault Mageau Stephen Mageau Carmen Elois Welch Mageau Vrana Los Angeles Attack at Blue Rock Springs Park

items.title: Chester Klingel Jr. promoted to message center chief in US Army
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Mon, Mar 13, 1950
items.searchText:
Chester Klingel Jr. promoted to message center chief in US Army Suspect Event Via <https://www.strictly-gi.com/wwii-mos-database/674-message-center-chief/>

# **Message Center Chief (674)**
# **_Enlisted Personnel_**
(Specification Serial Number 667 will be used for Message Center Chief assigned to units below a division.)
Directs and coordinates all phases of message center operations at a divisional or higher level to insure the transmission of all messages with the utmost speed, accuracy, and security. Supervises and instructs message center personnel in the function of a message center and in the performance of individual assignment.
Must thoroughly understand the use and relative advantages and limitations of the various means of communication as they pertain to security, transmission speed, traffic capacity and current effectiveness for the type of message to be transmitted.
Must be able to encipher and decipher, or encode and decode classified messages and understand the operation of all cryptographic equipment and cryptographic systems peculiar to the unit. Must have cryptographic clearance. Chester Clark Klingel Jr. Guam Military Cryptography

items.title: Paul Stine graduates 8th grade
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Fri, Jun 5, 1953
items.searchText:
Paul Stine graduates 8th grade Victim Event **Jamestown Will Graduate Ten**
_McClatchy Newspapers Service_

**JAMESTOWN —** The graduation exercises at Jamestown Elementary School will be conducted at 8:15 PM Friday when a class of 10 eighth graders receives diplomas.

The graduates will be addressed by Rev. Edgar Nelson, pastor of Sonora Methodist Church.

The valedictory address will be given by Mary Jasper, daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Edward M. Jasper of Jamestown.

**The program follows:**
Processional march; invocation, Rev. Nelson; Pledge of Allegiance, led by Ernestina Meza and Peter Oehr; _The Star Spangled Banner_, sung by the audience; salutatory address, Louise Nau; baritone solo, Mary Jasper; song, _You’re A Grand Old Flag_, graduating class; address to the graduates, Rev. Nelson; presentation of American Legion school award by Carlos D. Arellano; presentation of class, Orville Nichols, principal; presentation of diplomas, Jay Gibbs, member of the board of trustees; benediction, Rev. Nelson; recessional.

**The graduates include:** Glennon Bass, Timothy Bode Jr., Melvin Dale Cooper, Richard Raymond Gurley Jr., Louella Arline Hart, Mary Elizabeth Jasper, Peter John Lehr, Ernestina Meza, Louise Marie Nau, and **Paul L. Stine.** Paul Lee Stine Jamestown, CA Jamestown CA Paul Stine's Taxi Murder

items.title: Paul Stine wins Lions Club speech contest
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Mon, Mar 5, 1956
items.searchText:
Paul Stine wins Lions Club speech contest Victim Event **Paul Stine Is Contest Winner**

**HUGHSON —** Paul Stine, Hughson High School junior and the son of Mrs. Audra Stine, was the winner in the Lion’s Club speech contest before the meeting of the club Thursday evening.

Also speaking on the subject _“Youth Problems Tomorrow”_ were Rosalie Lewis and Marlene LeVake. A fourth contestant, Martin Thompson, was ill and unable to appear.

Judges were attorneys R. O. James and Howard Jones and Phil Ashmun. Milton Alvine and Ken Galt were timers and Elmer Owen and Ora Sheldon the tellers. The students were coached by David Steinberg of the school faculty.

Stine is now eligible to take part in the zone contest to be held in Riverbank March 14.

It was reported at the meeting that the sale of the Club community calendars will soon be underway. Paul Lee Stine Audra Thelma (Busby Stine) Harrison Hughson, CA Hughson CA Paul Stine's Taxi Murder

items.title: Gary Poste is in car accident
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Fri, Jan 9, 1959
items.searchText:
Gary Poste is in car accident Suspect Event On January 9, 1959, Gary Poste was involved in a serious car accident near Numa, Parke County, Indiana. The incident resulted in the death of his companion, 20-year-old Robert A. McManus, while Poste survived with significant injuries, including a fractured leg and broken jaw. 

_Article transcript follows._

**Friday, January 9, 1959**
### **HIGH SPEED CRASH KILLS AIRMAN, 20**
A 20-year-old airman was killed and a companion was injured early today in a high-speed auto accident at Numa in Parke County.

Dead is **Robert A. McManus**. Suffering a fractured leg and broken jaw is **Gary Poste**, 20. Both were stationed at an Air Force installation at Rockville. Their home towns were not immediately identified.

Police said the car, driven by McManus, left U.S. 41, bounced off the west side of a C. & E. I. Railroad underpass and slammed into the east side of the underpass.

The crash occurred at 3:15 a.m. (CDT).

Yesterday, a 70-year-old South Bend man died of injuries suffered four days earlier when his auto went out of control and struck a tree.

Police said **Roy S. Young** may have become ill before the accident. His death was the first traffic fatality in St. Joseph County this year. Gary Francis Poste Numa, IN Numa IN

items.title: Paul Stine "bumps" sleeping soldier
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Sun, May 31, 1959
items.searchText:
Paul Stine "bumps" sleeping soldier Victim Event **Motorist Nudges Snoozer At Millerton Lake**

The day was warm, the sand inviting, so Richard Vance Hotson, 20, an airman stationed in Madera, stretched out for a snooze at Millerton Lake.

A few minutes later Hotson was surprised to feel something nudging him uphill.

He discovered a car driven by **Paul Lee Stine, 19, of 1018 Vasser Avenue**, bumping him. Stine said because of the incline and his car being pointed uphill he failed to see the sleeping Hotson.

Hotson suffered bruises and abrasions but was not hospitalized. The incident happened on the Fresno side of the lake. Paul Lee Stine Fresno, CA Fresno CA Paul Stine's Taxi Murder

items.title: Paul Stine interns for Democratic campaign
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Sun, Jul 19, 1959
items.searchText:
Paul Stine interns for Democratic campaign Victim Event **Paul Stine Is Spending Summer In Politics**

**HUGHSON** — Paul Stine of Hughson and Howard Hobbs of Fresno are two Fresno State College students who will be learning the inside of politics under internships established by the Citizenship Clearing House of California.

Stine, a Democrat, has been assigned to the Democratic County Central Committee to aid Bert Delotto, a Fresno County Supervisor campaigning for assemblyman from the 32nd Fresno district, and Charles B. Garrigus, who is seeking to unseat the incumbent assemblyman from the 33rd district.

**THE YOUTHS** will receive $50 a week for 10 weeks. The clearinghouse, with a grant from the Ford Foundation, is providing half the money and the central committees the remainder. Each will work beside his candidate, learning what is involved in political campaigning.

In a quote from Dr. Karl Falk, head of the college social science division, the boys “will learn how to ring doorbells and make individual vote solicitations, will learn precinct organization work, will learn how speaking engagements are sought and how they are filled and how press releases are prepared.”

“**THE ONLY** requirement is that they get a good cross section of a local political campaign and not be assigned some routine task. A similar program is in effect in Los Angeles County. The two are parts of a pilot program and, if successful, may result in much wider application of the ‘political internship’ arrangement in coming years.”

**BOTH YOUNG** men feel they may enter the political field themselves in the future.

Stine, 18 years old and in his first year at F.S.C., will be unable to vote for his candidates in November, but states, “I plan to make up for that, and more, by ardent campaigning.”

His work is in addition to a part-time job as a dishwasher in a drive-in restaurant, to finance his way through college.

Before graduating from Hughson Union High School in 1957, Stine worked in the peach harvest and was a sports writer for the school, representing the _Turlock Journal._ Last summer he was employed full time for the _Journal_ as sports editor. Paul Lee Stine Turlock, CA Fresno County, CA Turlock CA Fresno County Paul Stine's Taxi Murder

items.title: Gary Poste accidentally shot
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Thu, Oct 13, 1960
items.searchText:
Gary Poste accidentally shot Suspect Event **Shooting Victim Improved; D.A. To Check Case**

Gary F. Poste, 22-year-old Fortuna man reportedly accidentally shot by a police officer friend Thursday night at the latter’s home in that city, was reported “greatly improved” at Redwood Memorial Hospital today.

Poste still remains on the critical list, however, until danger of possible complications is past.

Meanwhile, the district attorney’s office indicated it will conduct an investigation of its own into the matter. Deputy District Attorney James McKittrick stated he was certain that, due to “peculiar” circumstances, it will be checked into by D.A. investigators.

Poste was shot with a .357 caliber magnum revolver held by patrolman Robert Foster, 24, a member of the Fortuna Police Department for only two months. Police Chief Dale L. Livingston said the shooting was obviously accidental, adding that the victim had himself said so yesterday morning. Gary Francis Poste Fortuna, CA Fortuna CA

items.title: Alfred Hitchcock "Museum Piece" episode airs
items.subtitle: Cultural Event • Tue, Apr 4, 1961
items.searchText:
Alfred Hitchcock "Museum Piece" episode airs Cultural Event As noted originally on [ZodiacKillerFacts.com](https://zodiackillerfacts.com/zodiac-theories/zodiac-theories/zodiac-and-alfred-hitchcock-presents/), this episode features a character using a gun with a flashlight attached. It also features a line of dialogue: _“I remember the excitement of the manhunt. The most dangerous game.”_

Episode description via [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents_season_6):

> Mr. Hollister (Gates) is curator of a small museum that displays trinkets and human remains. He is visited by Newton B. Clovis (McCormick) who claims to be an archaeo-psychologist. They peruse through Hollister's collection before focusing on one particular skeleton, which Hollister said that he knew and who Clovis says was an athlete. Hollister tells him that the museum's collection was made by his son, Ben (Convy), who died after being erroneously convicted of first-degree murder. Clovis determines that Ben was kind and caring. 
> A flashback details how Ben hunted down a [gray fox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gray_fox) in a neighbor's barn while lovers Tim McCaffrey (Gilleran) and his girlfriend (Tompkins) watched. Tim struck Ben down with a pitchfork, and Ben's gun fired when he fell, killing Tim. Hollister was angry that District Attorney Mr. Henshaw (Platt) did nothing to stop the newspaper and the public from predetermining Ben to be guilty of murder. Henshaw used Ben's marksmanship (hitting the eye with every shot) against him while the judge (Meredith) struggled to maintain order in the courtroom and the defense attorney (Bradley) struggled to keep Ben from consistently committing outbursts. Henshaw also refused to help Ben get any leniency on his sentence, and Ben would be dead within two months after losing the will to live. 
> After their discussion of the events, Clovis examines the skeleton's teeth. It is revealed that the modern skeleton in the museum belongs to Henshaw, and Clovis is investigating that murder as a member of the district attorney's office. When Clovis turns away to look at the skeleton, Hollister stabs and kills him. 
> The episode ends as Hollister takes another group on a tour of the museum, this time examining both Henshaw's and Clovis' skeletons.  United States of America Cultural References

items.title: Earl Van Best arrested for "child stealing"
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Wed, Aug 1, 1962
items.searchText:
Earl Van Best arrested for "child stealing" Suspect Event **Jail Parts Child Bride, Husband, 28**
Love came to Earl Van Best, 28, in a San Francisco ice cream parlor.
“It was love at first sight,” said Van Best from San Francisco City Prison where he is held in lieu of $10,000 bail on charges of child stealing.
The center of his affections is at the Youth Guidance Center as a runaway. She is 14.
Best, who says he makes a living by selling rare books and documents he brings to the U.S. from Mexico, claims he married her in Reno Jan. 5.
But the girl’s parents got the marriage annulled.
The couple just returned from a trip through the Midwest, Southwest and Mexico. They were taken into custody at her parents’ home Monday. His case is continued in Municipal Court until next Tuesday so that he may obtain a lawyer. He said she is pregnant. Earl Van Best, Jr. Earl Van Best Jr. San Francisco

items.title: Earl Van Best & child bride are on the run
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Fri, Aug 31, 1962
items.searchText:
Earl Van Best & child bride are on the run Suspect Event ## **Ailing Child Bride, Spouse Disappear**
8/31/1962

The ailing child bride of Earl Van Best, 28, disappeared from her San Francisco General Hospital room early today, clad only in a short hospital gown.

Van Best, free on $10,000 bail on child stealing charges, also was found missing from his San Francisco apartment at 514 Noe St.

Police issued an alert to watch for the lovers after the girl’s mother suggested he may have aided in her escape.

The 14-year-old girl, reportedly pregnant, was taken to the hospital suffering from infectious hepatitis, a liver ailment.

Dr. Mary McAnaw of the hospital staff said the ailment has been partially arrested but that she could suffer a relapse unless she is under medical supervision.

First taken to the Youth Guidance Center as a runaway, she was transferred to the hospital about 10 days ago. Mrs. Harris said she was in bed at 4 a.m. but missing at 8 a.m.

The ill-fated romance of Earl Van Best, a rare book salesman, and the school girl blossomed in a San Francisco ice cream parlor.

He called it “love at first sight.”

Their Reno marriage in January subsequently was annulled by the girl’s parents. They were taken into custody at the home of her mother, Mrs. Verda Mae Kitli [Kitty/Kitt], at 1245 7th Ave., San Francisco, late in July after a trip through the Midwest, Southwest and Mexico. The girl had gone there to pick up some clothes.

## **Car Gives Clue in Child Bride Hunt**
### **Bulletin Out for Arrest of Pair in ‘2nd Elopement’**
9/1/1962

A 14-year-old girl, who presumably eloped a second time with her 28-year-old “rare book dealer” husband today was called back to San Francisco for a variety of reasons.

She apparently bears none of them.

Neither does Earl Van Best, 28, a book salesman, with whom she is believed to have spent the most recent and parental interruption.

Detectives want the girl to continue the hepatitis treatment she was getting earlier at San Francisco General Hospital. She disappeared early yesterday, clad only in a hospital gown. Officers want Van Best on child-stealing and other charges.

And her mother, Mrs. Verdanne Kittiman, of 1245 7th Ave., San Francisco, wants the girl back.

A statewide bulletin was issued for the arrest of Van Best and the girl, said to be four months pregnant, and for a yellow 1961 Falcon they were seen in in Cabillo Way at 514 Noe St., before they again vanished.

The couple met in an ice cream parlor last fall and eloped to Reno, where a January marriage was annulled by the girl’s parents. However, the pair disappeared and turned up a month later at the mother’s house after a “honeymoon” through the Southwest and Mexico.

Van Best was arrested on a child-stealing charge, freed on bail, and his case was continued in court. The girl was made a ward of the Juvenile Court and sent to the Youth Guidance Center as a runaway.

She later was transferred to San Francisco General Hospital when she became ill.

Dr. Mary McAnaw said the girl’s ailment had improved under antibiotics and subject to relapse. Earl Van Best, Jr. Earl Van Best Jr.

items.title: Earl Van Best sentenced for "Ice Cream Romance"
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Wed, Aug 14, 1963
items.searchText:
Earl Van Best sentenced for "Ice Cream Romance" Suspect Event **Tucson Daily Citizen**
**August 14, 1963**

**Ice Cream Romance Ends In Jail**

**SAN FRANCISCO (AP)** —
A love story that began in an ice cream parlor has ended with a 28-year-old man in jail for statutory rape and his 15-year-old sweetheart in a home for wayward girls.

The story got its start about a year ago when Earl Van Best Jr., a book salesman, and blonde Judy Chandler, then 14, met and fell in love in Herbert’s Sherbet Shoppe.

They ran away to Nevada, got married—and then Judy’s family found out. The marriage was annulled and Best was jailed for child stealing.

He was freed on bail but helped Judy escape from a hospital where she was ill with hepatitis. Once again, they fled. In April, they were found in New Orleans, where—police said—Best had wrapped Judy’s newborn child in a towel and left it on a doorstep. The child later was adopted by foster parents.

Best was sentenced to a year in San Francisco County Jail yesterday. A one- to 50-year state prison sentence was suspended, and a child-stealing charge dropped. Earl Van Best, Jr. Earl Van Best Jr. San Francisco New Orleans, LA New Orleans LA

items.title: Darlene Ferrin leaves home with Jim Phillips / Crabtree
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Thu, Nov 25, 1965
items.searchText:
Darlene Ferrin leaves home with Jim Phillips / Crabtree Victim Event **Page 59: CRIME REPORT SUPPLEMENT - VALLEJO POLICE DEPARTMENT**
- **CRIME:** 187 PC
- **CLASSIFICATION:** Murder
- **DATE:** 7-4-69
- **VICTIM'S NAME:** Ferrin, Darlene Elizabeth
- **ADDRESS:** 1300 Virginia Street
- 1-23-70 11:30 AM
- RO again contacted mother of victim, Mrs. Suennen, 130 Jordan St., re- any possible information she might have which would connect her ex-son-in-law with the death of her daughter as she is alleged to have given to Joseph DeLouise. Mrs. Suennen offered nothing new in way of factual information. Subject is admittedly emotionally upset over daughter's death and further admits that she disliked son-in-law, James Phillips. She could offer very little background information on Phillips and was not in fact around him for any great period of time. Mother stated Darlene had left the family home on Nov. 25, 1965. This happened after a family dispute. The family did not hear from her again until Oct. 1966 when she returned home with her husband, James D. Phillips. Mother has marriage license which shows couple was married in Reno, Nevada, Jan. 1, 1966. When they came to her home Darlene told them she and her husband had just returned from the Virgin Islands where they had spent five months. Phillips told Mrs. Suennen that he "owned" a newspaper in the Virgin Islands and that he was formerly in the U. S. Army as an undercover agent. Subject had no money, no income, no job, and lived off his wife's earnings as a waitress. Couple lived with wife's family. After approximately three weeks Phillips was told to either obtain employment and support his wife or move out. He at this time applied for employment at the Daily Republican Newspaper in Fairfield and was in fact
- **REPORTING OFFICERS:** Mulanax, Sgt. Darlene (Suennen) Ferrin Jim Phillips Crabtree Darlene Ferrin's parents home (1969) Attack at Blue Rock Springs Park

items.title: Friends notice Cheri Jo Bates' car is in her driveway
items.subtitle: Case Event • Sun, Oct 30, 1966
items.searchText:
Friends notice Cheri Jo Bates' car is in her driveway Case Event > At approximately 3:00 p.m. and again at 3:45 p.m., Cheri called her close friend Stefani whom she asked to accompany her to the RCC library “to pick up a few books.”
> However, Stefani was unable to join her. A short while later, sometime between 4:30 and 5:00 p.m., friends of Cheri’s drove past the Bates’ house and observed Cheri’s distinctive vehicle still parked in her driveway. In 1966, the RCC library had unusual Sunday hours of operation in that it was open from 1:00 to 5:00 p.m. and again from 6:00 to 9:00 p.m. In other words, the library was closed during the 5 o’clock hour.
> Another friend thought she saw Cheri heading toward campus in her Volkswagen around 6:15 p.m. 3 Four fellow students who knew Cheri entered the library at about 7:15 p.m. and stayed until closing. None of the four saw Cheri at any point during their time in the library. But at least one of the four had noticed her easily recognizable car parked on Terracina Drive, just outside the library.2,4 In fact, no one interviewed by police could say, definitively, that he or she had seen Cheri in or around the RCC library on the night of her murder. 
> The closest anyone came to confirming Cheri’s presence was an unnamed male librarian who said he “thought he saw” Cheri during the Sunday-evening session. Unfortunately, he couldn’t provide an exact time.
Cole, Michael. [The Zodiac Revisited, Volume 1: The Facts of the Case](https://www.amazon.com/Zodiac-Revisited-Facts-Case/dp/0996394303) (p. 168). Cheri Jo Bates Home of Cheri Jo Bates The Murder of Cheri Jo Bates

items.title: Cheri Jo Bates checks out three books at the library
items.subtitle: Case Event • Sun, Oct 30, 1966
items.searchText:
Cheri Jo Bates checks out three books at the library Case Event Transcript of October 20, 1969 memo from Riverside Police Chief to Napa Police Chief Earl Randol:

> DEPARTMENT OF POLICE
> L T KINKEAD
> CHIEF
> 
> INCORPORATED 1883
> CITY OF RIVERSIDE
> CALIFORNIA
> 
> RADIO STATION KMB 401
> 
> October 20, 1969
> 
> REF: 352-481
> 
> Earl Randol
> Sheriff of Napa County
> Napa, California 94558
> 
> Attention Captain Donald A. Townsend
> 
> [Stamp: RECEIVED Oct 22 10 59 AM '69 SHERIFF'S DEPT. NAPA COUNTY]
> 
> Dear Sir:
> 
> This letter is in reference to our telephone conversation of 10-17-69 regarding the similar M.O. of your "Zodiac" suspect and the suspect of our homicide File No. 352-481.
> 
> It may aid you to have a brief synopsis of our homicide. It is as follows:
> 
> On October 30, 1966, Cheri Jo Bates, a college student at Riverside City College, was brutally murdered. Our investigation revealed that the victim had gone to the city college campus to obtain some books from the library (the library was open on Sunday for the students' benefit). It was established that she had entered the library and checked out three books at approximately 6:00 PM. She returned to her vehicle, which was parked on a city street a short distance from the library, placed her books in the vehicle, and attempted to start her vehicle. The vehicle had been tampered with so it would not start. This was evidently done by the suspect to keep the victim near her vehicle so that the suspect could make his approach.
> 
> Our victim then left her vehicle, accompanied by the suspect, and walked approximately 200 feet from her vehicle into a dirt driveway between two houses. These houses were vacant and a part of the school property, having recently been purchased by the city college. While in this driveway area, our victim was attacked with a knife and stabbed numerous times in the chest. She was also stabbed once in the back, and her throat was severly cut, almost to the extent where she was decapitated. In addition to the stab wounds, our victim had been beaten about the
> 
> [Handwritten note: 10-24-69 Phoned Det Dorrington Gave info on Meneley - Narlow]
> 
> ADDRESS ALL COMMUNICATIONS TO THE CHIEF OF POLICE - BOX 88
> 
> Sheriff Earl Randol
> Page 2
> October 20, 1969
> 
> face and had been choked. There was no evidence that the victim had been sexually attacked, as she was fully clothed and the clothing was not disarranged, There was nothing to indicate a motive of robbery, as our victim's purse and its contents were intact. From all indications, the knife used by the suspect was one of approximately a 1/2-inch width blade by 3-1/2 inches long.
> 
> One month after the homicide, letters were received at the Press and our department written by the suspect of our homicide. The suspect used a black felt pen to address the envelopes and had used upper case print. The confession letter was typed. There are numerous errors in spelling, punctuation, etc., as you will notice.
> 
> The person who wrote the confession letter is aware of facts about the homicide that only the killer would know. There is no doubt that the person who wrote the confession letter is our homicide suspect.
> 
> The original of this letter was evidently destroyed or kept by the suspect, as the Press and our department received a carbon copy of the original. These carbon copies were a fourth or fifth copy and difficult to read. A photograph of this letter and the envelopes is attached. A reproduction of the confession letter is also attached. It should be noted that the copies received by the Press and our department were on plain white paper of poor quality. Width of the paper is 8 inches. The length of the paper is unknown, as the suspect (a peculiarity) tore off the bottom and top of the paper. (Refer to the photographs.)
> 
> It might be worthwhile to note that just outside the city limits of Riverside is located March Air Force Base, a SAC base. Physical evidence found at the scene of our crime indicated that heel prints found near the body were made by a heel that was manufactured for military and other government agencies, including prisons.
> 
> We were able to lift some latent fingerprints from the victim's vehicle. These prints were not identified. Our unidentified prints are on file with the FBI under the FBI File No. 32-27195, Latent Case No. 73096. Copies of the latent lifts from your homicide were obtained from CII and sent to the FBI for comparison with the latent lifts of our investigation.
> 
> There are numerous similarities in your homicide and our Inv. 352-481. I thought you should be aware that we are working a similar-type homicide investigation. If you are able to determine by
> 
> Sheriff Earl Randol
> Page 3
> October 20, 1969
> 
> handwriting comparison or by any other means that your homicide suspect is the same as ours, please advise. I will notify you of the results in comparing your latent lifts with ours as soon as I hear from the FBI.
> 
> I hope this information may aid you in your investigation. Please be assured of our complete cooperation in all matters of mutual interest.
> 
> Yours very truly,
> 
> L. T. KINKEAD
> Chief of Police
> 
> H. L. Homsher, Sergeant
> Detective Division
> 
> HLH:seb
> 
> Attachments: 1. Two reproductions of the
> confession letter.
> 
> 2. Photograph of the envelope
> received by the Press.
> 
> 3. A photograph of the envelope
> received by our department.
> 
> 4. Two Xerox copies of envelopes
> received (after having been
> processed for fingerprints).
> 
> Cy to: Chief of Police, San Francisco Police Department Cheri Jo Bates Riverside City College Library

items.title: Ronald Allen marries Karen Long
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Sat, Nov 26, 1966
items.searchText:
Ronald Allen marries Karen Long Suspect Event **SAN MARINO RITES**

**Karen Long Weds Ronald Gene Allen**

Of interest to their many local friends is news of the marriage of **Ronald Gene Allen**, the son of Cmdr. and Mrs. E. W. Allen of Vallejo, and **Karen Marie Long**, the daughter of Mrs. James Wilcox Long of San Gabriel. The marriage was solemnized on **November 26 at 8 o’clock in the evening** at the **San Marino Congregational Church**, with the **Rev. Bertrand R. Crist** officiating.

The bride was given in marriage by her grandfather, **Alfred R. Dumas**. Her _lovely_ gown of ivory brocade was designed by Gallina, in a simple A-line with a very full, long train falling from the shoulders. A large bow of the same fabric as the gown framed her face and her veil was of ivory silk illusion in cathedral length. Slippers of ivory satin with rosettes on the toes and wrist-length white kid gloves completed her bridal costume. She carried a presentation bouquet of small white roses.

**Miss Linda Cathcart of Burbank** attended as maid of honor, wearing a gown of gold faille in an A-line design with a panel back. A bow of the same gold faille with a double veil of gold illusion, shoulder-length, and matching slippers were her accessories.

The two bridesmaids, **Miss Mary Eilers of Upland** and **Miss Stephanie Hoffman**, a cousin of the bride from Corona Del Mar, wore identical gowns to that of the maid of honor and carried long-stemmed red roses.

Serving as best man was the groom’s brother, **Leigh Allen of Berson, California**, and ushers were **Jere French of Pomona** and **James Hoffman of Corona Del Mar**.

For her daughter’s wedding, Mrs. Long chose a gown of cinnamon-colored chiffon cut velvet with long sleeves gathered at the wrist and a round collar at the throat. She wore a cinnamon-toned stole hat and matching shoes and handbag; her corsage was of green orchids.

The groom’s mother wore a mint-green and orchid brocade jacket ensemble, with a small green satin hat and matching shoes; her flowers were a corsage of bronze cymbidium orchids.

A **reception** followed at the **South Pasadena Women’s Club**, where members of the wedding party and both families greeted guests. Assisting at the reception and in charge of the guest book was **Miss Jean Hoffman** of Corona Del Mar, cousin of the bride.

For her traveling costume, the new Mrs. Allen wore a textured white silk suit with thin black and gold stripes forming a plaid pattern. The couple planned to make their home in **San Francisco**, where the bride was attending **San Francisco State College**.

Mrs. Allen is a graduate of **California Polytechnic College** and spent her junior year at the **University of Stockholm in Sweden**. The groom is also a graduate of California Polytechnic College. Ronald Gene Allen Arthur Leigh Allen Karen Marie (Long) Allen San Marino, CA San Marino CA Arthur Leigh Allen

### Via Wikipedia:
> One of the potential clues supporting this theory was the discovery of a macabre poem scratched into the underside of a foldable desk in the RCC library.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murder_of_Cheri_Jo_Bates#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherwood2011138-41) This poem was discovered by a custodian six months after the murder and contains graphic references to repeated assaults upon young women with a bladed weapon. Titled "Sick of living/unwilling to die", the poem's language and handwriting resembled that of the Zodiac's letters. It was signed with what were assumed to be a set of lower case initials (_r h_).[[d]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murder_of_Cheri_Jo_Bates#cite_note-43) The desk in question was in the college storage area at the time the poem was discovered, although the custodian informed police the desk had been on the library floor at the time of Bates's murder. Police photographed the inscription and added this piece of [circumstantial evidence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circumstantial_evidence) to the case file.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murder_of_Cheri_Jo_Bates#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFlaherty19936%E2%80%937-38) Cheri Jo Bates Riverside Community College Riverside, CA Riverside CA Non-Canonical Crimes The Murder of Cheri Jo Bates

items.title: Linda L. Gillinger abducted and released in San Diego
items.subtitle: Zodiac Related Events • Wed, Dec 7, 1966
items.searchText:
Linda L. Gillinger abducted and released in San Diego Zodiac Related Events **Kidnap-Murder Connection Is Being Probed**
Riverside homicide detectives last night were investigating the possible connection between the abduction Wednesday of a 19-year-old girl and the Oct. 30 murder of Cheri Jo Bates.
The girl, Linda L. Gillinger, was released unharmed in San Diego, after her abductor forced her to drive there at gunpoint after waiting for her in the back of her car.
She described her kidnaper as a young, college - looking man with short brown hair and medium build. Miss Bates was stabbed to death on the Riverside City College campus by a man who waited by her car.
Miss Gillinger told detectives the man tried to kiss her, but she slapped him and he slapped her back. He ordered her out of the car, locked it, and threw the keys away.
"Now you have as good a chance as I do," the man said. They both ran in opposite directions from the car. She reached the San Diego Police Department about 2 a.m.  Riverside, CA San Diego, CA Riverside CA San Diego The Murder of Cheri Jo Bates

items.title: Paul Stine marries Claudia Wescott
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Wed, May 31, 1967
items.searchText:
Paul Stine marries Claudia Wescott Victim Event **Claudia Wescott Marries**
Cargill Methodist chapel was the setting for the candlelight ceremony Saturday night at which Claudia Stark Wescott and Paul L. Stine repeated vows after Dr. Carl Stromberg.
 Mrs. Clouden Wescott, 1511 E. Holmes St., is the bride’s mother and Mrs. Roy Harrison, Modesta, Calif., is the mother of the bridegroom.
The bride’s saki A-line dress, with empire bodice was worn beneath a floor-length split coat with venise lace elbow sleeves. Her waist-length illusion veil was held by a venise lace band accented with stephanotis. The bride carried a bouquet of gardenias, stephanotis and lilies of the valley.
Carol Ann Gissler, San Francisco, Calif., the bride’s roommate, was honor attendant and Nancy Scott, Madison, bridesmaid. They wore blue crepe A-line dresses with venise lace yoke and sleeves. Their ensembles were completed with head circlets of blue and white daisies and lilies of the valley.
David James, Cincinnati, Ohio, was best man and Donald Cripps, usher.
The buffet supper reception was held at the Monterey Hotel before the couple left for San Francisco. They will honeymoon in the coastal regions of California and Oregon and reside at 1842 Fell St., San Francisco.
The bride, a Janesville High School graduate, received her B.A. degree from the University of Wisconsin. She is a statistician at State of California Workmen’s Compensation Insurance Fund. Her husband, who is affiliated with American United Life in San Francisco will return to San Francisco State College for graduate study in English in the fall. Paul Lee Stine Claudia (Wescott) Stine Janesville, WI Janesville WI

items.title: Darlene and Jim Phillips Crabtree divorce official
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Fri, Jun 2, 1967
items.searchText:
Darlene and Jim Phillips Crabtree divorce official Victim Event **Page 60: CRIME REPORT SUPPLEMENT - VALLEJO POLICE DEPARTMENT**
- **CRIME:** 187 P.C.
- **CLASSIFICATION:** Murder
- **DATE:** 7-4-69
- **VICTIM'S NAME:** Ferrin, Darlene Elizabeth
- **ADDRESS:** 1300 Virginia St.
hired to assist in writing editorials. Mrs. Suennen stated he worked in this capacity for approximately three weeks. (Phillips in fact worked five days and did not show up for work again.) Shortly after leaving the paper both Darlene and Phillips left Vallejo and went to Pennsylvania. They spent several months in Pennsylvania and returned to Vallejo. Their marriage was going poorly and Darlene decided to go to Reno and get a divorce. James apparently did not want a divorce but did not contest same. Records show final decree obtained in Reno on June 2, 1967. Shortly afterwards Darlene remarried and Phillips moved to San Francisco. As far as family knows no further contact between Darlene and Phillips except she contacted him in San Francisco to have him sign some papers pertaining to their divorce. Family had no further contact with Phillips until he attended the funeral of Darlene and was seen by two members of the family sitting in the back row during funeral services. He was with an unknown WFA who was pregnant at the time. There was no conversation between Phillips and family.
Mr. DeLouise had indicated to RO that Mrs. Suennen had told him that Phillips was at one time committed to a mental hospital. RO asked her if this was a fact and Mrs. Suennen stated she had "heard" this but did not know it to be a fact. She was also questioned concerning any particular interest Phillips might have had in astrology or cryptograms. Stated she had on one occasion seen him draw up some type of "code".
- **REPORTING OFFICERS:** Sgt. Mulanax
**Page 61: CRIME REPORT SUPPLEMENT - VALLEJO POLICE DEPARTMENT**
- **CRIME:** 187 P.C.
- **CLASSIFICATION:** Murder
- **DATE:** 7-4-69
- **VICTIM'S NAME:** Ferrin, Darlene Elizabeth
- **ADDRESS:** 1300 Virginia St.
- **1-23-70 2:30 PM:** RO contacted Business Manager of the Daily Republic in Fairfield in an attempt to secure more background information on Phillips and if possible secure printing specimens of his writing. Very little known of Phillips by present employees. No record of his application for employment. An attempt to be made by newspaper to locate federal tax forms made out by Phillips to authorize tax withholding deductions. If located same will be forwarded to the Vallejo P.D. for possible handwriting comparison.
- **3:00 PM:** Checked with Fairfield PD re-any record they might have of Phillips. They have an outstanding warrant charging violation 476a P.C. on file since 12-4-67. Also obtained mug shot which was attached to warrant of a James Donald Phillips and a photostatic copy of prints which, it is assumed, came from CII. It is opinion of RO that subject shown is not the James Phillips in which we are interested. Subject appears older in photograph and does not wear glasses.
- **1-26-70 9:30 AM:** Contacted Mel Nicolai, CII Investigations, by phone. Requested he search his files for any record of a James D. Phillips, WMA, approx. 24-28, and forward mug and rap sheet if found.
- **REPORTING OFFICERS:** Sgt. Mulanax Darlene (Suennen) Ferrin Jim Phillips Crabtree  Attack at Blue Rock Springs Park

items.title: Vallejo coverage of Deer Lodge escapees being recaptured
items.subtitle: Media Event • Mon, Jul 22, 1968
items.searchText:
Vallejo coverage of Deer Lodge escapees being recaptured Media Event An actual prison escape happened at Deer Lodge prison a little more than a year prior to the Lake Berryessa incident where Zodiac _may_ have referenced the prison. This article could have been the source of the obscure reference either to Zodiac or the transcribing officer.

Transcription of [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/image/1102906309) article:

> KIDNAP, SHOOTING
> Escapees Caught After Violence
> 
> SANDPOINT, Idaho (AP) — Two Montana Prison escapees were captured in woods near this North Idaho resort community Sunday — but not before they had threatened a couple out camping with a knife to get their pistol and car, left them bound and gagged and shot another man after kidnaping him and taking his car, sheriff's deputies said.
> 
> One of the convicts, shivering as law officers surrounded him and his campion, said he fell into Lake Pend Oreille while fleeing officers and was "too cold" to keep running. The two offered no resistence although they still carried the stolen pistol.
> 
> The Bonner County Sheriff's office here said David Finch, 28, and Raymond Lee Wilson, 27, were in jail here. The two were among 12 who fled a prison farm near the Montana penitentiary at Deer Lodge July 15. Three of the inmates were still at large.
> 
> CAMPERS ATTACKED
> Deputies said Mr. and Mrs. Richard Yates, Bonners Ferry, Idaho, were camped north of Bonners Ferry when two men approached claiming they were lost. When the couple became suspicious, the convict pulled a knife, bound and gagged the pair and took their .22 caliber pistol and car. Near Sandpoint, the Yates' car ran out of gasoline and the two convicts commandeered a car belonging to Michael Winslow, Sandpoint, and forced him at gunpoint to drive toward Sandpoint. An argument developed at a service station between the convicts and Winslow over the pumping of gasoline, deputies said, and Winslow was shot in the forearm. The convicts then fled in the wounded man's car.
> 
> POLICE CHASE
> The two convicts were spotted by Sandpoint City Policeman Willard Piehl who gave chase and alerted other officers. State police and sheriff's deputies responded and captured the escapees after they abandoned the Winslow vehicle and fled into woods near the lake.
> 
> Winslow's wound was minor and the Yates were not hurt, deputies said. Vallejo Times-Herald Deer Lodge Prison Deer Lodge, MT Vallejo, CA Deer Lodge MT Vallejo CA Attack at Lake Berryessa

items.title: BayCon 1968
items.subtitle: Cultural Event • Thu, Aug 29, 1968
items.searchText:
BayCon 1968 Cultural Event Suspect Paul Doerr has been sighted (unconfirmed) in the crowd.

The **26th World Science Fiction Convention** ([Worldcon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Worldcon)), also known as **Baycon**, was held on 29 August–2 September 1968 at the Claremont Hotel in [Berkeley, California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkeley,_California), United States.
In 1968, [Worldcon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Worldcon), annual World Science Fiction Convention, was combined with [Westercon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Westercon), the annual West Coast Science Fantasy Conference, sharing guests of honor and chairmen. The chairmen were [Bill Donaho](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bill_Donaho&action=edit&redlink=1), Alva Rogers, and [J. Ben Stark](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J._Ben_Stark&action=edit&redlink=1).
Participants
Attendance was approximately 1,430.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/26th_World_Science_Fiction_Convention#cite_note-smith-1)
**Guests of Honor**
- [Philip José Farmer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philip_Jos%C3%A9_Farmer) (pro)
- Walter J. Daugherty (fan)
- [Robert Silverberg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Silverberg) (toastmaster)
Awards
_Main article: [Hugo Award](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award)_
The World Science Fiction Society administers and presents the [Hugo Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award),[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/26th_World_Science_Fiction_Convention#cite_note-wsfshugo-2) the oldest and most noteworthy award for science fiction. Selection of the recipients is by vote of the Worldcon members. Categories include novels and short fiction, artwork, dramatic presentations, and various professional and [fandom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science_fiction_fandom) activities.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/26th_World_Science_Fiction_Convention#cite_note-wsfshugo-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/26th_World_Science_Fiction_Convention#cite_note-bs771030-3)
Other awards may be presented at Worldcon at the discretion of the individual convention committee. This has often included the national SF awards of the host country, such as the Japanese [Seiun Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seiun_Award) as part of [Nippon 2007](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nippon_2007),[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/26th_World_Science_Fiction_Convention#cite_note-otherawards-4) and the [Prix Aurora Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prix_Aurora_Awards) as part of [Anticipation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anticipation_(convention)) in 2009. The [Astounding Award for Best New Writer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astounding_Award_for_Best_New_Writer) and the [Sidewise Award](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sidewise_Award_for_Alternate_History), though not sponsored by the Worldcon, are usually presented, as well as the [Chesley Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chesley_Awards), the [Prometheus Award](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prometheus_Award), and others.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/26th_World_Science_Fiction_Convention#cite_note-otherawards-4)
**1968 Hugo Awards**
- [Best Novel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Novel): _[Lord of Light](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lord_of_Light)_ by [Roger Zelazny](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roger_Zelazny)
- [Best Novella](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Novella):
    - "[Weyr Search](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dragonflight_(novel))" by [Anne McCaffrey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anne_McCaffrey) and
    - "[Riders of the Purple Wage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riders_of_the_Purple_Wage)" by Philip José Farmer (tie)
- [Best Novelette](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Novelette): "Gonna Roll Them Bones" by [Fritz Leiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fritz_Leiber)
- [Best Short Story](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Short_Story): "[I Have No Mouth, and I Must Scream](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I_Have_No_Mouth,_and_I_Must_Scream)" by [Harlan Ellison](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harlan_Ellison)
- [Best Dramatic Presentation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Dramatic_Presentation): "[The City on the Edge of Forever](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_City_on_the_Edge_of_Forever)" (_[Star Trek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series)_ episode, original script by Harlan Ellison)
- [Best Professional Artist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Professional_Artist): [Jack Gaughan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jack_Gaughan)
- [Best Professional Magazine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Professional_Magazine): _[if](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/If_(magazine))_
- [Best Fanzine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Fanzine): _Amra_, edited by [George Scithers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Scithers)
- [Best Fan Artist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Fan_Artist): [George Barr](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Barr_(artist))
- [Best Fan Writer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Fan_Writer): [Ted White](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ted_White_(author))
**Other awards**
- Special Award: Harlan Ellison for _[Dangerous Visions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dangerous_Visions)_
- Special Award: [Gene Roddenberry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_Roddenberry) for _[Star Trek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Trek)_
Notes
This Worldcon was the first one that was attended by large numbers of people in the [hippie subculture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hippie_subculture). A [psychedelic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychedelic_art) [light show](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquid_light_show) was presented in the main ballroom of the hotel. [Robert Silverberg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Silverberg) gave a speech about using [LSD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LSD) to attain a [mystical experience](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mystical_experience). [Harlan Ellison](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harlan_Ellison) read a story he had written about a hippie commune.
[Poul Anderson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poul_Anderson) emceed a presentation by the [Society for Creative Anachronism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Society_for_Creative_Anachronism).
Long-time president of [DAW Books](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DAW_Books), [Betsy Wollheim](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betsy_Wollheim) (whose father, author and publisher [Donald A. Wollheim](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_A._Wollheim), was a leader in the development of science fiction as a popular genre) remembers:
> The high point of my childhood experience at conventions was Baycon 1968 in Oakland. I remember the excitement of it all—sliding down the fire slides, the big eucalyptus forest behind the Claremont Hotel...the tournaments on the lawn.... And I remember at the age of 16 thinking, "No convention can ever be this wonderful again, so I should never go to another." Famous last words![[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/26th_World_Science_Fiction_Convention#cite_note-locus-5) Paul Alfred Doerr, Jr. Society for Creative Anachronism Paul Alfred Doerr Jr. Berkeley, CA Berkeley CA Renaissance Fairs

items.title: David Faraday leaves to pick up Betty Lou
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Fri, Dec 20, 1968
items.searchText:
David Faraday leaves to pick up Betty Lou Victim Event David left his home around 7:10 p.m. and drove his sister to a meeting of the Rainbow Girls at the Pythian Castle. At 7:20, he returned home and his mother gave him $1.50 in change for the date. David left ten minutes later and arrived at the Jensen house where he was greeted by Betty Lou’s father, Verne, and her mother, Virginia. Betty Lou wore a purple dress with a white collar and cuffs. David wore brown corduroy pants, a light-blue, long-sleeve shirt, and low-cut brown boots. David and Betty Lou told Mr. and Mrs. Jensen they were going to a festival at Hogan High. David’s sister, Debbie, later recalled him saying they were going out to Lake Herman Road to hang out with some other kids. The two teenagers promised to be home by 11:00 p.m. and climbed into the Rambler to begin their night together.

Butterfield, Michael. The Zodiac Killer: The Story of America's Most Elusive Murderer (p. 11). (Function). Kindle Edition. David Arthur Faraday Betty Lou Jensen Pythian Castle (Vallejo, CA) Verne Marion Jensen Virginia Lee (Blenderman) Jensen Pythian Castle (Vallejo CA) Home of David Faraday Home of Betty Lou Jensen Murders at Lake Herman Road

items.title: Gasser and Donnelly go hunting, see white Impala
items.subtitle: Case Event • Fri, Dec 20, 1968
items.searchText:
Gasser and Donnelly go hunting, see white Impala Case Event ### **Page 21**
**OFFICE OF SHERIFFCounty of Solano, California**
**OFFENSE REPORT**
**OFFENSE:** 187 P.C. Murder **CASE NO:** V-25564 **RO:** LUNDBLAD & BUTTERBACH **STATEMENT:** ROBERT [redacted], WMA age 27, 1088 [redacted] Avenue, Napa, CA (Employee of PG&E, Vallejo 224-[redacted]) FRANK GASSER, WMA age 69, Gasser Ranch, Highway 21 North of Goodyear Station
[redacted] and GASSER have been identified by Deputy Villarreal as racoon hunters who were in the area of the Benicia Pumping Station and who have been identified by MRS [redacted]. They had their 1959 pick-up truck, Red in color, with wood side-boards, parked inside the field of the Marshall Ranch.
In this interview it was learned that they were hunting in the area from 9:00PM to 11:00PM. 11:00PM was approximate, because [redacted] looked at his watch and he knew it was in the area of 11:00PM. It could have been five minutes to the hour or ten minutes to. It took them three or four minutes, possibly five minutes to get back to their truck, walking up the road on the

### **Page 22**
**SOLANO COUNTY SHERIFF'S OFFICE** **FAIRFIELD, CALIFORNIA** **SUPPLEMENTAL INVESTIGATION REPORT**
**CASE NUMBER:** V-25564
sides of the creek. They arrived at the truck and shortly a car pulled into the driveway, which was later identified as belonging to [redacted]. They claimed that they left the area several minutes after [redacted], who established the time as 11:20, that they left the scene. They described the light colored 1960 Rambler S/W that was parked at the gate. It was parked Southwest of where we finally found the car. This discrepancy wasn't noted at first. This was a statement as to their activity during that night. They said that when they arrived there at 9:00PM a white 4-door hardtop, a '59 or '60 Impala, was parked there, and also, a truck coming out of the gate. This coincides with INFORMATION from BINGO WISENER that when he came out of the gate he saw the same Impala and also saw the red pick-up truck go by.
SEE OTHER REPORT OF ROBERT [redacted]. (Det/Sgt Lundblad & Butterbach)
### **Page 23**
**OFFICE OF SHERIFF** **County of Solano, California**
**OFFENSE REPORT**
**OFFENSE:** 187 P.C. Murder **CASE NO:** V-25564 **RO:** LUNDBLAD & BUTTERBACH **STATEMENT:** ROBERT M. [redacted], 1088 [redacted] Avenue, Napa, CA (DOB 5/14/[redacted])
SECOND INTERVIEW: Due to some discrepancies or misunderstandings in the first interview on 12/21/68.
MR [redacted] was asked to pin-point his activies closer on that day. He states that he went to the Gasser Ranch at approximately 6:00PM. They sat around awhile and didn't leave until several hours later. They arrived in the area of the pump station around 9:00PM. They parked the car about 25 feet inside of the Marshall Ranch property off of the road. They walked the creek towards the pump station. They had the dogs loose and they treed either a cat or a racoon in the area of the Dotta Ranch. While they were there they saw some sort of activity up there, all the lights were on, but they were a little distance away and couldn't tell what the activity was. The dogs treed a racoon in an oak tree near the Pump Station. The racoon was shot with
### **Page 24**
**SOLANO COUNTY SHERIFF'S OFFICE** **FAIRFIELD, CALIFORNIA** **SUPPLEMENTAL INVESTIGATION REPORT**
**CASE NUMBER:** V-25564 **RE: [redacted] SECOND INTERVIEW**
a long barrell .22 revolver. [partially illegible]
[redacted] remembers looking at his watch around 11:00 PM or five or ten minutes before and they headed back towards the car. He said it took them about 10 minutes to walk back to the truck. He was at the truck about a total of 5 minutes and a car came in, that has been identified as MRS [redacted]'S, it drove in, turned around and drove back in the opposite direction towards Benicia, again. He states that approximately 5 minutes later they drove in his truck a '59 Chevrolet, red in color, with white wood sideboards, cattle guards, on the sides. They drove this truck back to the Gasser Ranch heading towards Benicia and they passed the car. [redacted] insists that the Rambler was parked on the bank. That would be on the South side. He did not see any person in the car. He estimates he left the area between 11:00 and 11:15PM. He remained at the Gasser's house about an hour and then left out through Highway #21, through the Jamison Canyon and headed home, arriving about 12:30AM.

[redacted] was asked if he had a rifle. He stated that he had a automatic rifle. The RO advised [redacted] of his constitutional rights at this time. Due to the fact that he was in the area and he did have a rifle which he claimed he
### **Page 25**
**SOLANO COUNTY SHERIFF'S OFFICE** **FAIRFIELD, CALIFORNIA** **SUPPLEMENTAL INVESTIGATION REPORT**
**CASE NUMBER:** V-25564 **page 3**
had with him that night. He was given a receipt for the rifle. The rifle was tested at the S/O by Rangemaster George Parks. On the basis of the findings of George Parks this rifle has been eliminated as a suspected weapon. [redacted] states that FRANK GASSER does own an automatic rifle but that he doesn't have it at the house. He suggested that we call or see a gentleman by the name of HARLAN [redacted] who lives at 106 Marquis Avenue. It is possible that the gun is there, because GASSER does not leave his guns at the ranch because of recent thefts. [redacted]'s car has California license D-26024.
(Det/Sgt. Lundblad & Butterbach) Frank Gasser Robert Connelly Lake Herman Pumping Station Murders at Lake Herman Road

items.title: Faraday / Jensen leave Sharon's house
