items.title: "Sam" doesn't show up at Fairmont Hotel
items.subtitle: Media Event • Wed, Oct 22, 1969
items.searchText:
"Sam" doesn't show up at Fairmont Hotel Media Event **TV ‘Zodiac’ Reneges on Surrender**
_By Hubert J. Bernhard_

The hunt for the “Zodiac” killer focused on the Fairmont Hotel this afternoon in the wake of a television show that featured a man who represented himself as the mass slayer.

Nob Hill’s famed hostelry entered the picture after a mysterious caller fenced for two hours with questioners on the call-in show while a huge Bay Area audience watched.

The caller promised to meet a prominent attorney later in the morning and surrender. He failed to keep the rendezvous—but police were reported to have a suspect in which they were “strongly interested”—possibly as a result of the aborted meeting.

**Hotel Pickup**
It was after that that the Fairmont loomed as a key setting in the investigation.

Taxi driver Paul Stine, 29, the “Zodiac” killer’s latest victim, is believed to have picked up the man who killed him on the street in the vicinity of the Fairmont.

During the television broadcast this morning, the “voice” who repeatedly called the show told attorney Melvin Belli to meet him on the roof of the Fairmont—alone.

Bryan Hartnell, 20, who was wounded in the Lake Berryessa knife attack in which Cecilia Ann Shepard was killed by the “Zodiac” suspect, later listened to a replay of the entire television show.

When the tape came to the point where the caller mentioned the Fairmont, Hartnell asked to have it replayed. It was—repeatedly—for more than 20 minutes.

“Meet me on top of the Fairmont Hotel with nobody else, or I’ll jump,” the voice said.

**No Recognition**
Hartnell gave no outward signs of recognition, but that was the only portion of the tape he asked to hear again.

Hartnell, one of three persons who heard the replay in a sealed room, was asked about a possible identification of the voice afterward.

“I can’t say a word at this time,” he said. “We’re not sure. It’s too short to tell. We’re going to have another conference.”

During the television show, a waitress in the Blum’s candy shop at the Fairmont telephoned to report that a man who resembled circulated pictures of “Zodiac” had stopped in for a cup of coffee.

She reported that she had put the cup and saucer aside for examination by police fingerprint experts.

The television show—springboard for a sequence of new developments in the suspenseful search for the slayer—was arranged by police.

Capt. Martin Lee, chief of inspectors and head of San Francisco’s “Zodiac squad,” said police had no assurance that the mysterious caller was actually responsible for the five “Zodiac” murders.

“I think the caller has a problem,” he said, “but whether he’s involved in the killings, I cannot say.”

Lee said the strange television performance was set up after a man telephoned “a neighboring police department” about 11 p.m. last night and offered to negotiate.

**Sincere**
He was taken seriously, Lee explained, because he “seemed to have knowledge and he seemed sincere.”

San Francisco police did not talk to him directly, but they acceded to his request to have an attorney on the television show this morning to attempt to arrange a surrender.

Melvin Belli, one of two lawyers the caller asked for by name, appeared on Jim Dunbar’s regular KGO-TV morning talk show as a result.

A series of telephone calls ensued, at the end of which Belli—off the air—made a 10:30 date to meet the mystery man at St. Vincent de Paul’s Thrift Shop, 672 S. Mission Street.

**Broke Date**
He and Dunbar went there at the appointed time, and police arrived half an hour later when it appeared the caller had no intention of keeping the date.

Belli told reporters he hoped the man would telephone his office, saying he was “a very sick person and needs help.”

The rendezvous was arranged after Belli and Dunbar received 15 telephone calls during the two hours they were on the air.

Most were obviously from the same person, although several seemed to be in a different voice. Belli expressed concern that someone else was entering the dialogue.

The caller represented himself as suffering from headaches and twice declared he had the urge to “kill again.”

**Different Voice**
But _The Examiner_ learned that the police officer who took the original call that led to the broadcast said the voice on the Dunbar program was apparently not that of the man to whom he spoke.

During the broadcast, in which the Dunbar show was kept on the air more than half an hour past its normal cut-off time, one San Francisco police inspector described the odds they were dealing with as “like flipping a coin.”

**No More Calls**
Dunbar and Belli opened the conversation with the self-described killer by clearing the switchboard to accept no other calls than his at 7 a.m.

Police promised they would make no effort to trace the calls. The first call was at 7:14 a.m., during a commercial.

The audience heard Belli, when the commercial ended, saying:
“Did you hear me?”
“Yes, I did,” replied the caller in a soft, almost boyish voice.

He hung up, apparently because he was nervous. But he called back a few minutes later.

**Call Him ‘Sam’**
When Belli pleaded with him for a better name to address him by than “Zodiac,” the caller agreed to be referred to as “Sam.”

He asked a second time for a Fairmont rooftop meeting, setting no specific time for the rendezvous.

Dunbar and Belli assured him that the lines were not tapped, and that no attempt was made to trace the calls. They tried, too, to persuade him to say what police department he had called as evidence that he was indeed the man who initiated the conversations.

Dunbar and Belli tried to value “Sam” to identify the police department he’d contacted in order to determine whether they were dealing with the same man.

He parried their questions, however, and they never asked about the five murders charged to “Zodiac.”

Belli assured the caller of a fair trial and repeatedly asked whether he wanted the attorney to contact District Attorney John J. Ferdon with a promise that no effort would be made to send him to the gas chamber.

**Headaches**
The caller once said he suffered from “headaches,” adding that aspirin would not help. Then he hung up, and frequently during the strange sequence he said he was getting one again.

When he called again, Belli asked how long he had suffered from headaches.
“Ever since I killed that kid,” the caller replied.
“Was that before last December?”
“Yes.”

December was the month in which the first of the murders attributed to “Zodiac” took place.

Once when the caller hung up, he did so declaring: “I don’t want to give myself up. I want to kill those kids.”

Another time, he said as he hung up: “I’m getting one of my headaches now. I’ve got to kill. I’ve got to kill.”

Several times he made groaning sounds which explained by saying, “That’s my headache speaking.”

Belli finally promised he would contact the district attorney’s office about leniency, and then he and Dunbar ended the show after agreeing to meet the caller “inside” at Daly City.

District Attorney Ferdon said later that his office had not been contacted by Belli, but that he would make no deals.

“There’s nothing we will promise,” Ferdon said. “We are in no position to make promises to anyone named Zodiac. We don’t know whether Belli is representing this voice or not.

“When the evidence is brought before us, then we will make a decision as to our proceedings.”

**No Evidence**
The mysterious caller said nothing during the television show that could be taken as evidence he was indeed the man sought by officers of four jurisdictions.

Police in San Francisco and Vallejo, together with the Napa and Solano County sheriff’s offices, meanwhile set up a “hot line” for instant communication on the “Zodiac” case.

All departments have been involved in the investigation of murders attributed to the killer.

The close contact resulted from Monday’s meeting here to exchange every scrap of evidence and information involving the three murders in Vallejo lovers’ lanes, one at Lake Berryessa and the latest here.

The killer who calls himself Zodiac has boasted of the murders in cryptograms and letters sent to _The Examiner._

Meanwhile, Chief Inspectors Lee confirmed of slugs taken from victims yesterday that ballistic tests and shell casings show that the killer used three and possibly four different weapons in his reign of terror.

It was known previously that a .22-caliber pistol was used to shoot and kill Betty Lou Jensen, 16, and her boyfriend, David Faraday, 17, in a lovers’ lane near Vallejo last Dec. 20.

A 9-mm pistol killed Darlene Ferrin, 23, and critically wounded Michael Mageau, 19, in another lovers’ lane at Blue Rock Springs Park in Vallejo last July 5.

A knife with a six- to eight-inch blade took the life of Miee Shepard, who was stabbed more than 20 times, and critically wounded her companion, Hartnell, who has recovered from 12 stabs.

A 9-mm pistol took the life of Stine, 29. The slug taken from his body was too smashed to make a comparison with those in the Vallejo murder. It may or may not have been the same gun. Sam / Eric Phillips Weill Melvin Belli Fairmont Hotel Martin Lee John J. Ferdon San Francisco

items.title: Dr. D.C.B. Marsh dares Zodiac to reveal name
items.subtitle: Media Event • Wed, Oct 22, 1969
items.searchText:
Dr. D.C.B. Marsh dares Zodiac to reveal name Media Event # **Cipher Expert Dares Zodiac To ‘Tell’ Name**
_By Will Stevens_

A challenge perhaps unique in the annals of American crime was injected today into the bizarre case of the killer who begins his letters to newspapers: “This is Zodiac speaking.”

The challenge came from Dr. D.C.B. Marsh, president of the American Cryptogram Association, and one of the world’s distinguished cryptographers.

A scholarly, soft-voiced professor of mathematics at Colorado School of Mines in Golden, Colo., Dr. Marsh told _The Examiner_ today:

“The killer wouldn’t dare—as he has claimed in letters to the newspaper—to reveal his name in a cipher to established cryptogram experts.”

### **Incomplete Truth**

“He has, to quote Edgar Allen Poe, that ‘any cipher devised by man can be solved by man.’”

“Zodiac” has not told the complete truth in his cipher messages to _The Examiner_, _The Chronicle_, and _The Vallejo Times-Herald,_ Marsh suggested.

“Zodiac” has not done this, Marsh suggested, because to tell the complete truth in relation to his name—in cipher code—would lead to his capture.

### **Original Claim**

A modest scholar, Dr. Marsh then laid down this challenge:

“I invite ‘Zodiac’ to send to _The American Cryptogram Association,_ care of Dr. D.C.B. Marsh, president, at 4164 Mt. Golden, Colo., 80401, a cipher code—however complicated—which will, when solved, honestly include his name—for study by myself and my colleagues in the association.”

Since the Dec. 20 night “Zodiac” shot to death the first of five victims—a 16-year-old boy and girl in a lovers’ lane near Vallejo—he has mailed intricate code messages in cipher to _The Examiner,_ _Chronicle,_ and _Times-Herald._

He claimed, in his original cipher message, that it revealed his identity.

Most of the code was deciphered, subsequently, by Donald G. Harden, a Salinas high school teacher, and his wife.

That message, as decoded by Harden, began:

“I like killing people because it is so much fun it is more fun than killing wild game.”

But there were 18 garbled letters at the end—never deciphered.

Today, Dr. Marsh told _The Examiner:_

“That first code was complicated and the man obviously knew what he was doing.”

But it never, Dr. Marsh said, included the true identity of “Zodiac.” San Francisco Examiner Donald C. B. Marsh American Cryptogram Association San Francisco The 340 Cipher The 408 Cipher

items.title: SF Chronicle: "Cops No Closer on Zodiac Identity"
items.subtitle: Newspaper Article • Sat, Oct 25, 1969
items.searchText:
SF Chronicle: "Cops No Closer on Zodiac Identity" Newspaper Article Cops No Closer on Zodiac Identity 
By Paul Avery
Homicide detectives in San Francisco, Vallejo and Napa said they are no closer to catching the cryptic killer than they were ten months ago - unless some clue to his identity is among the hundreds of leads still in the process of being checked out. Zodiac struck last on October 11 when he gunned down cab -driver Paul Stine on Washington street in Presidio Heights. He revealed himself the killer in a letter sent to The Chronicle three days later. Since then he has remained silent.
Without exception, all the investigators assigned to the case feel Zodiac will eventually be caught. "We'll get him," said one. "We may not know his name at the moment -but we know a lot about him and what we know will finally lead us to him." Such confidence is based on several factors. There are at least four witnesses who have seen Zodiac when he wasn't wearing the black hood which, like the crossed circle symbol he signs letters with, has become a Zodiac hallmark. There are three persons who have heard his voice.
There are samples of his handwriting, and fingerprints found at murder scenes which may be his, and tire tracks, and other evidence authorities won't disclose. Sooner or later, detectives believe, tthey will find a man who matches the killer's descriptions who will be further identified as Zodiac by eyewitnesses, his voice, evidence, and, possibly, by his own admission. Paul Avery San Francisco Chronicle San Francisco San Francisco Chronicle offices

items.title: Las Vegas Checks Man As ‘Zodiac’
items.subtitle: Case Event • Wed, Oct 29, 1969
items.searchText:
Las Vegas Checks Man As ‘Zodiac’ Case Event **Las Vegas Checks Man As ‘Zodiac’**
A possible suspect in the “Zodiac” assault and murders is being checked out in Las Vegas, it was learned last night.
Police there arrested a Los Angeles man on a firearms charge and promptly notified the San Francisco police department.
Clark County sheriff’s detective said Wednesday that Gary Ronald Schilling, 27, was arrested on the Las Vegas strip Tuesday night for possession of an unregistered firearm.
Fingerprints and handwriting samples have been forwarded to San Francisco police for investigation in the Zodiac murders.
Detectives said they probably wouldn’t have an reply before today at the earliest.
No hint was given as to why police thought this man to be connected with the weird Zodiac affair.  Las Vegas, NV Las Vegas NV

items.title: Schilling released; Toschi doubts as suspect
items.subtitle: Case Event • Thu, Oct 30, 1969
items.searchText:
Schilling released; Toschi doubts as suspect Case Event On October 28–30, 1969, Las Vegas police arrested **Gary Ronald Schilling**, 27, of Gardena (or Los Angeles), California, after finding him asleep in his car on the Las Vegas Strip. Deputies discovered a **.380 Beretta** and a **.22 caliber pistol** in the vehicle and charged him with possession of **unregistered firearms**. Because the calibers matched those used in the Zodiac killings, Schilling was briefly detained on a **murder hold** as a potential suspect in the Zodiac case.
Fingerprints and handwriting samples were sent to **San Francisco Police** for comparison, but investigators—including **Inspector Dave Toschi** of the Zodiac task force—quickly expressed skepticism. Toschi described Schilling as “one of about 5,000 suspects who resembled our composite,” adding that “it’s just a routine check. Somebody jumped the gun on this and it’s gotten completely out of hand.”
Other SFPD officials noted that Schilling’s features did not match the Zodiac composite sketch and that there was “nothing exceptional about this suspect.” Officers emphasized that the similarities in weapon caliber were coincidental and that Schilling did **not** resemble the Zodiac.
Authorities confirmed that Schilling was also wanted in **Downey, California**, for a loitering charge. After questioning by Las Vegas detectives, he was released when no connection to the Zodiac murders was found.

////////

**Another False Alarm In Zodiac Case**
SAN FRANCISCO (UPI) — Homicide investigators said today a man being held in Las Vegas as a possible suspect in the Zodiac murders “doesn’t look like anything that we’re interested in at all.”
Inspector Dave Toschi, one of the team assigned to the Zodiac case, said Gary Ronald Schilling, 27, Los Angeles, was “one of about 5,000 suspects who resembled our composite (picture) of Zodiac that we’ve checked.”
“We can’t positively rule him out until we receive his fingerprints,” the inspector said. “But it’s just a routine check. Somebody jumped the gun on this and it’s gotten completely out of hand.”
Schilling was arrested on the Las Vegas Strip Tuesday night for possession of unregistered firearms. He was later booked on a hold for murder as a possible suspect in the five Bay Area killings of recent months.
Toschi said Schilling had been questioned at length about the Zodiac killings by Las Vegas detectives and didn’t seem to be a likely suspect.
Schilling was arrested when deputies found him sleeping in the back of his car in the parking lot of a hotel. They found a .380 beretta and a .22 caliber pistol in the car.
Fingerprints and handwriting samples were sent to San Francisco police to determine if there is any connection between the man and the so-called Zodiac killer.
Authorities in San Francisco have attributed five murders in recent months to a person who has called himself Zodiac in letters to police and newspapers boasting of the murders.

**SUSPECT HAD TWO GUNS**
An Inglewood man, who does not resemble the composite sketch of Zodiac, psychotic slayer of five persons, is being checked out as a possible suspect because he had two guns of the same caliber as the ones used by Zodiac, San Francisco police said today.
The fingerprint and handwriting comparisons have not yet been completed, an officer said.
Gary Ronald Schilling, 27, was picked up for investigation after Las Vegas police found him sleeping in his car and armed with a .22 caliber pistol and a foreign-make 9 mm. automatic, police reported.
One officer indicated skepticism about the case, saying the San Francisco police have checked dozens of similar suspects during their Zodiac investigation. “There’s nothing exceptional about this suspect,” the officer said. “And his features do not match those in the composite drawing.”
In discussing Zodiac, the San Francisco officer said that although the murderer is unique in devising the cryptogram and contacting police after his slayings, he is not necessarily a trained cryptographer.
“He could have gone to any library and gotten a book to help him create the three-part cipher,” the officer said.

**Suspect Said Not Zodiac**
SAN FRANCISCO (UPI) — Homicide investigators said today a man being held in Las Vegas as a possible suspect in the Zodiac murders “doesn’t look like anything that we’re interested in at all.”
Inspector Dave Toschi, one of the team assigned to the Zodiac case, said Gary Ronald Schilling, 27, Los Angeles, was “one of about 5,000 suspects who resembled our composite (picture) of Zodiac that we’ve checked.”
“We can’t positively rule him out until we receive his fingerprints,” the inspector said. “But it’s just a routine check. Somebody jumped the gun on this and it’s gotten completely out of hand.”
Schilling was arrested on the Las Vegas Strip Tuesday night for possession of unregistered firearms. He was later booked on a hold for murder as a possible suspect in the five Bay Area killings of recent months.
Toschi said Schilling had been questioned at length about the Zodiac killings by Las Vegas detectives and didn’t seem to be a likely suspect.

**Zodiac Suspect Probed**
LAS VEGAS (UPI) — A man arrested on a firearms charge has been booked on a hold for murder in California and is being checked out as a possible suspect in the San Francisco Zodiac murders.
Clark County sheriff’s detectives said Wednesday that Gary Ronald Schilling, 27, Gardena, Calif., was arrested on the Las Vegas Strip for possession of unregistered firearms.
The car in which he was arrested was registered at 14517 S. Vermont, Gardena.
Los Angeles police said Schilling was also wanted in Downey, Calif., on a loitering charge.
Schilling was arrested when deputies found him sleeping in the back of his car in the parking lot of a hotel. They found a .380 beretta and a .22 caliber pistol in the car.
Fingerprints and handwriting samples were sent to San Francisco police to determine if there is any connection between the man and the so-called Zodiac killer.
Authorities in San Francisco have attributed five murders in recent months to a person who has called himself Zodiac in letters to police and newspapers boasting of the murders.
San Francisco police said they had received information from Las Vegas authorities but added that their investigation of the suspect was not complete. Gary Ronald Schilling David Toschi San Francisco Las Vegas, NV Las Vegas NV

items.title: Children urged to skip Trick 'R Treat
items.subtitle: Case Event • Thu, Oct 30, 1969
items.searchText:
Children urged to skip Trick 'R Treat Case Event **ON HALLOWEEN**
 **Children Urged To Stay At Home**
Traditional “Trick or Treat” by small fry in Vallejo residential areas on Halloween are being discouraged this year by Vallejo Chief of Police **Jack E. Stiltz**, and by **Dr. John W. Nicoll**, superintendent of the Vallejo Unified School District, because of the threat posed by the “Zodiac Killer.”
Dr. Nicoll said Wednesday notices are being sent to parents of all children in the pre-school through sixth grade years, saying he endorses Chief Stiltz’ request that trick or treat activities be discontinued this year.
“Although notices are not being sent to parents of junior and senior high school students, I nevertheless urge these parents to take a good look at the situation and follow the police chief’s advice,” Dr. Nicoll added.
The school notice to parents reads:
 “The Vallejo Unified School District is cooperating closely with the Vallejo Police Department in an endeavor to protect our children from the menace of the so-called Zodiac Killer. Accordingly, I wish to endorse Chief Jack E. Stiltz’ request that the traditional trick or treat activities be discontinued this year, particularly with young children.
“To quote Chief Jack Stiltz: ‘I realize that in asking for this cooperation from the parents, that many young people who have been looking forward to this annual trick and treat fun will be disappointed. I regret it very much, but I feel that it is too great a risk to take when a killer is at large, such as the Zodiac Killer,’” the superintendent’s notice concluded.
Several days ago, after claiming his fifth victim since last Dec. 20 — four in Solano and Napa counties — Zodiac wrote the San Francisco police department his next target might be a school bus. Accordingly, school buses have been guarded since, on their runs to and from school.
No attempts have been made on school buses since the threat. Jack E. Stiltz John W. Nicoll SF Bay Area

items.title: Suspicious "Aroused" letter to Modesto Bee re: Joe Stine
items.subtitle: Newspaper Article • Mon, Nov 3, 1969
items.searchText:
Suspicious "Aroused" letter to Modesto Bee re: Joe Stine Newspaper Article ### Transcription
**“Very Brave Man”**

_Editor of The Bee — Sir:_ After reading the article _“Modestan Offers To Be Killer’s Victim”_ on the front page of _The Bee_ Oct. 23, I would just like to say Joe Stine is a very brave man. He has put his life in jeopardy to save others. He has said he will not be carrying any weapon and will further be riding a bicycle to and from his job. There is a very good chance the Zodiac killer will take Stine up on his offer and how will he protect himself?

Every night I read the paper and watch young men on the news demonstrating against the war in Vietnam. I cannot help but feel they could not possibly think it is immoral as many of them do not know the meaning of the word as they smoke their pot and advocate free love. They are just not man enough to fight, no matter what the cause. Stine has proved he is a man and a very brave one at that. There are not enough around.

I attended Hughson High School with Joe Stine’s brother, Paul Stine, one of the Zodiac’s victims, and feel we have lost a very fine person. I hope we can show Joe Stine and his mother we care. I am sending a small donation to help start a fund to buy Stine an automobile for the cold wet days ahead and I hope many more will join in. Just imagine what $1 from each reader could do.

I am sending my small check to Muriel’s Richfield Service Center, 706 Sutter Ave., Modesto.

**Modesto.**
**AROUSED.** Joe Charles Stine Modesto, CA Modesto CA Paul Stine's Taxi Murder

items.title: Dr. Marsh interviewed in Colorado about Zodiac and cryptograms
items.subtitle: Media Event • Fri, Nov 14, 1969
items.searchText:
Dr. Marsh interviewed in Colorado about Zodiac and cryptograms Media Event **Golden Cryptographer Decodes Messages Sent by Zodiac Killer**
_By Bob Duncan_

There is an admitted killer running loose in San Francisco who claims to have taken the lives of seven human beings, although police can link him with only five. He apparently enjoys taunting police by sending cryptograms about himself and his murders. Authorities have stated publicly they believe the man to be sane. There are those who doubt he is.

The killer calls himself Zodiac and evidently enjoys reading news of himself in the press as he sends ciphered (coded) messages to that medium. His age is guessed at 35 to 45. Two sketches exist which might be likenesses of the man and authorities have circulated these in hopes of someone spotting him.

Zodiac has threatened to flatten the tires of school buses and “pick off the kiddies as they come bounding out.” Either the man did not intend carrying out this threat or police presence aboard school buses scared him off as he did not kill any of the children—yet.

Dr. Donald G. B. Marsh, a mathematician at the Colorado School of Mines, has been asked by the San Francisco police department to decipher the cryptograms (coded messages). Dr. Marsh has been in the deciphering game since the age of 16 when his first interest and interest in ciphers first drew him when he first saw the film serial “The Mastermind of Mr. Moto.” He has been a member of the Association for 30 years and estimates approximately 4000 others share his tremendous solution of over 40,000 “crunchy” objects. Since 1954 he has edited the foreign cipher section of the Association’s journal and was elected President of the Association in 1964 and is now serving his second term in that office. Although now he is involved with Zodiac, Dr. Marsh states, “My most pleasant satisfying accomplishment in the field to date has been solving a cipher (written in French) by the fabulous Casanova. So far as I can determine, this chapter will only be enjoyed by the late William Friedman, one of the greatest cryptologists, who, although never published his solution.”

Dr. Marsh, a modest man, is probably one of the most highly respected persons living in cryptography. Though he may not boast of his successes, the mere fact that the San Francisco police sought him at all is proof of his esteem and reliability.

Thoroughly modest, Dr. Marsh is aware of his capabilities and has issued a challenge to Zodiac to send him a code that would reveal his true identity. Dr. Marsh says he can identify the man by the type of cipher he uses.

The latest message from Zodiac has yet to be forwarded to Dr. Marsh for study. He speculates that insurance by the police department may delay the transfer of immediate area.

Because of involvement in many other code deciphering mysteries including one that is as well known as San Francisco police, Dr. Marsh has a special privilege of knowing the mystery killer’s method of encoding. He says that the most important thing to know about a cipherer is his personality.

During an interview with Dr. Marsh, he talked about how he became involved with the case.

“On the evening of August 5th, 1969, I received a telephone call from Will Stevens, staff writer of _The San Francisco Examiner._ After introducing himself and asking me if I had heard about the killing, he told me that the killer had taken place in the vicinity of Vallejo, California.”

During a long discussion with Will Stevens, he supplied me with the information I had not heard concerning Zodiac. I began my study of the ciphers. Then Stevens told me that Donald Harden and his wife, a school teacher in Salinas, had worked on the ciphers and solved them.”

“I was sent the first cipher which the killer refers to in his messages as ‘This cipher is my name.’”

Dr. Marsh explained further that he found the cipher to be “A variation of the Broken Rail Fence,” a form of transposition in which letters of the message are rearranged—but the message remains the same.

Marsh knew the type of mind he was dealing with—practical and systematic.

Copies of the ciphers reached me by mail a few days later and I began a study of them when I was shortly interrupted by another telephone call from San Francisco stating that Donald Harden of Salinas had reported solving the ciphers, saying that the message began, “I like killing people.” I was asked whether or not I could verify his solution. With this lead given me, I was able to recover the message within an hour, arriving at the same solution as Mr. Harden.

“Since this time, the _Examiner_ has very graciously kept me informed of developments in the case (including two further murders) and I have been interviewed and taped by a San Francisco ‘all-news’ radio station and a lieutenant of their Homicide Bureau. Further messages have been received by the police and news media, linking the same man to all five murders, but no further ciphers have been received (or, if received, they have not been released to the public).”

“As submitted to the newspapers, the August ciphers employed various geometric symbols and letters of the alphabet (some reversed, some inverted). The three portions incorporated the same symbols, indicating the likelihood that the same method of encipherment was used in each case. Each section consisted of these symbols neatly printed in 8 x 17 rectangular arrays. When deciphered the three, collectively, composed a single, rather wild, message. The killer did not explicitly reveal his name as promised, but the terminal 18 symbols, when transliterated consistently with the intelligible portion of the message, remain an enigma. They read, ‘E B E O R I E T E M E T H H P I T I.’ They could have been used merely as nulls (symbols to fill in a design, such as the rectangles employed by the writer); they could represent some secondary encipherment which has not yet been discovered; they could be an anagram, although this does not seem to conform with the other portion of the cipher.

“In my opinion, some errors of encipherment, as well as the use of the various symbols rather than simple letters or numbers, indicate that ‘Zodiac,’ as the murderer now refers to himself, is not an experienced cryptographer. Each symbol represents a unique letter of the alphabet, but nearly every letter of the alphabet was assigned more than one symbolic equivalent. These multiple substitutes and the omission of word spaces made the ciphers more difficult than simple substitution ciphers found in many newspapers and puzzle magazines, but it would not require an expert in cryptography to devise either of these complications.”

Dr. Marsh is currently working on the latest of Zodiac’s cryptograms which he read in a newspaper. Though he is not a policeman, Dr. Marsh is anxious toward the challenge of decoding the message and of being an aid to the police in bringing the killer to justice. Donald C. B. Marsh Golden Daily Transcript Golden, CO Golden CO The 340 Cipher

items.title: A (likely different) Louis Myers linked to burglary ring
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Sat, Jan 24, 1970
items.searchText:
A (likely different) Louis Myers linked to burglary ring Suspect Event **Vallejo Burglary Ring Broken, 10 Arrested**
**‘Thefts-On-Order’ Setup Took Goods Valued At $10,000**

Vallejo police juvenile officers announced Saturday the cracking of a “burglary by order” ring, and arrested two men and eight juveniles as participants in the operation in which loot worth at least $10,000 was stolen from 53 South Vallejo homes since November. Sgt. Harold Thacker, officer in charge of the Juvenile Department, said the suspects all were Caucasians. He and Officers Jim Husted and Lee Vaughn had been working on the case almost unceasingly for weeks.

“It’s one of the slickest operations we’ve ever come up against,” Husted said. “It wasn’t until we were able to develop a pattern of house burglaries in South Vallejo that we were able to solve it.”

As of Saturday afternoon, police had recovered approximately $3,500 worth of stolen goods.

**Two Charged**
The adults who were jailed were identified as Casimiro (Casey) Saavedra, 26, of 137 Jordan St., and Louis Myers, 19, of 1182 Lewis Ave. Both were charged with possession of stolen property. Saavedra was freed on bail of $2,000, and the same bail figure was set for Myers who remains in custody.

Husted said the men who were jailed owned the ring but hired the “little orders” for Saavedra, who allegedly advised them on the types of articles he wanted. “For example,” the officer said, “the youngsters, all in their mid-teen bracket, might be told to obtain an electric organ, a TV set, gun, or a watch.”

The “Fagin” involved in the operation even provided the accomplices with transportation to the homes that were to be robbed, Husted said.

**Thefts**
Husted said the series of burglaries began as long ago as last May.

“But the ring, as far as we can determine, wasn’t organized until November, then things really started rolling,” he added.

In a single block, he said, as many as five homes were burglarized during the three months the burglary ring was operating at its peak.

Most of the loot recovered was stored in Saavedra’s house, officers said. The suspect, however, denied any knowledge of the crime and told police the articles in his home were recovered by him after his home was burglarized.

The mystifying case was solved almost by happenstance, Husted said.

It was reported to him by a local school principal that a youth was attempting to sell a watch at school.

“We looked him up, saw the watch, and thought it was quite a bit more expensive a watch than a boy his age ought to have,” Husted went on.

Upon questioning, the boy admitted the watch had been taken in a house burglary, and implicated a second youth.

“One thing led to another, but it wasn’t all that easy,” Husted said. “For example, we had to research all the burglaries that had occurred in Vallejo in recent months to determine from the reports the methods used and the sort of merchandise that was taken.

“We think we have cleared up 53 house break-ins — there may be more.”

The youths that were booked either have been released in the custody of their parents, or are being detained in Juvenile Hall, Fairfield.

Thacker said the boys told him they were paid specified amounts for items stolen, such as $30 for the electric organ, $25 for a hunting rifle, and varying sums for jewelry, depending on its value.

“These kids were as clever as adult professionals,” Thacker added. “They always wore gloves so they wouldn’t leave any fingerprints, and they never carried burglar tools.”

They also never committed any acts of vandalism in any house they entered. Entry, incidentally, almost always was gained by smashing a window, or opening a screen.

“It’s a relief to have these burglaries cleared up,” Sergeant Thacker commented. “The whole department has been working night and day to solve them.”

No date has been set for the Municipal Court arraignments of Saavedra and Myers. The youths’ cases will be taken up in Juvenile Court. Louis (Louie) Joseph Myers Vallejo, CA Vallejo CA

items.title: SFPD Park Station bombing
items.subtitle: Other Crimes • Mon, Feb 16, 1970
items.searchText:
SFPD Park Station bombing Other Crimes Via Wikipedia:
> On February 16, 1970, a pipe bomb filled with [shrapnel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fragmentation_(weaponry)) detonated on the ledge of a window at the [San Francisco Police Department](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San_Francisco_Police_Department)'s [Upper Haight](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Upper_Haight) Park substation in San Francisco, United States.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San_Francisco_Police_Department_Park_Station_bombing#cite_note-jzsfc-1) Brian V. McDonnell, a police [sergeant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sergeant), was fatally wounded in its blast.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San_Francisco_Police_Department_Park_Station_bombing#cite_note-UPI_09-2) Robert Fogarty, another policeman, was severely wounded in his face and legs and was partially blinded.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San_Francisco_Police_Department_Park_Station_bombing#cite_note-3) In addition, eight other policemen were wounded.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San_Francisco_Police_Department_Park_Station_bombing#cite_note-jzsfc-1) The perpetrators were never caught. The prime suspect is [Bernadine Dohrn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bernadine_Dohrn) of the [Weather Underground](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weather_Underground).[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)_] 
> In 2009 District Attorney [Kamala Harris](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kamala_Harris) and San Francisco Police Chief Heather Fong enforced a gag order on the open investigation. The gag order request came from President [Barack Obama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barack_Obama)’s Attorney General [Eric Holder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eric_Holder) and the Justice Department.
> Dohrn was pointed out by fellow Weather Underground leader [William Ayers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Ayers) as the bomber during a WU Central Committee Leader meeting in Buffalo, New York in 1970.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)_]
> FBI informant Larry Grathwohl stated this account with Ayers, when he recalled that Ayers scolded leaders of the Cuban DGI and Soviet KGB backed organization for not doing more and making their foot soldiers carry out the bombings rather than the leaders.
> The bomb at Park Station was constructed the same way as the Greenwich Village townhouse explosion, in New York City, three weeks later in March 1970. That bomb was to be used at Fort Dix, New Jersey to kill Army personnel and their wives at a Dance at the Non Commissioned Officers Club. WU members died in the detonation. SFPD Golden Gate Park Police Station

items.title: The Kathleen Johns incident
items.subtitle: Possible Zodiac Crime • Sun, Mar 22, 1970
items.searchText:
The Kathleen Johns incident Possible Zodiac Crime On March 22, 1970, at approximately 11:15 PM, Kathleen Johns, a 22-year-old woman traveling with her 10-month-old daughter Jennifer, became involved in what would later be considered a possible Zodiac Killer encounter. This incident occurred on Highway 132 near Interstate 5 outside Patterson, California, approximately 72 miles east-southeast of Vallejo.
## The Abduction
According to Johns' report to the Patterson Police Department, she was driving from San Bernardino to Petaluma to visit her sick mother when a vehicle began following her, flashing its lights. After she pulled over, a man approached her car claiming her wheel was wobbling. He offered to tighten the lug bolts on her left rear wheel. After appearing to fix the problem, Johns attempted to drive away but discovered the wheel immediately fell off, secured by only one bolt.
The man then offered to drive Johns and her infant daughter to a service station. Once they entered his vehicle, however, he drove them around on back roads for approximately 1.5 hours. During this time, according to Johns' statement, the man mumbled about "killing people and throwing their bodies out of cars" and threatened to kill her and throw her baby out afterward.
## The Escape
When the vehicle stopped at an intersection, Johns seized the opportunity to escape. She grabbed her daughter and fled across a field, hiding in a ditch until she saw the suspect drive away. She then walked or hitchhiked approximately two miles to the nearest road where a passing motorist picked her up and brought her to the Patterson Police Department at around 2:30 AM.
## Police Investigation
Sergeant Charles J. McNatt of the Patterson Police Department documented Johns' arrival in a "hysterical condition." Johns described her abductor as a white male, approximately 30 years old, 5'9" tall, 160 pounds, with dark hair and complexion, wearing a dark windbreaker jacket and black slacks. She stated she could identify him if seen again.
Johns' vehicle, a brown 1957 Chevrolet station wagon, was later found completely destroyed by fire approximately two miles west of Interstate 5 and one mile north of Highway 132. Investigation revealed the lug bolts on the left rear wheel were missing, and the fire appeared to be of incendiary origin.
## Connection to the Zodiac Killer
While at the police station, Johns reportedly noticed a wanted poster with a composite sketch of the Zodiac Killer and identified him as her abductor. The San Francisco Police Department was contacted regarding the possible connection to the Zodiac case.
On July 24, 1970, the Zodiac Killer sent a letter to the San Francisco Chronicle in which he claimed responsibility for the incident, writing that he had "a little list" of victims which included "the woman + her baby that I gave a rather interesting ride for a coupple howers one evening a few months back that ended in my burning her car where I found them."
## Perspectives on the Case
The connection between Johns' abduction and the Zodiac Killer remains debated among investigators:
- **Supporting evidence**: The Zodiac's own claim of responsibility in his July 24 letter; Johns' identification of the composite sketch; the proximity to other Zodiac activity; and the threatening behavior consistent with the Zodiac's pattern.
- **Skeptical view**: Some investigators question the reliability of Johns' identification and note inconsistencies in her account. The location was also farther east than the Zodiac's confirmed attacks, which were concentrated in the San Francisco Bay Area.

The Kathleen Johns incident remains one of the most significant potential Zodiac encounters where the victim survived to provide a detailed account. Johns died of a heart attack in 2002, and the case continues to be studied as part of the broader Zodiac investigation. Kathleen Johns Zodiac Modesto, CA Hwy 132 Patterson, CA Modesto CA Patterson

items.title: Kathleen Johns reports incident to Patterson Police Dept.
items.subtitle: Case Event • Mon, Mar 23, 1970
items.searchText: Kathleen Johns reports incident to Patterson Police Dept. Case Event On the morning of 23 March 1970, at about 0230 hours, Mrs. Kathleen Johns was brought to the Patterson Police Department in a hysterical condition. When Mrs. Johns was quieted it was learned from her that a car had followed her from Modesto on Highway 132, and began blinking the lights in an attempt to get her to stop which she refused to do. Mrs. Johns states that after she had entered Interstate 5 that the vehicle then passed her, and at this time she stopped to check her car and was flagged to the wrong side of the road by a male subject who had been following her, had stopped, and had backed up the freeway. The man got out and advised her that the wheel on her car was wobbling and that he would fix it for her, that he began to take off a tire wrench and appeared to be tightening the lug bolts on her left rear wheel. The man then got into his own car and drove off without saying anything. Mrs. Johns then got into her own car and as she started driving off the wheel came off her car, whereupon the subject came back and said he would drive her to a service station. Mrs. Johns then got her infant into the car with the subject and he drove her around for what seemed to be an hour and a half or more, driving on back roads and stopping and turning around several times. She said he kept mumbling about killing people and throwing their bodies out of cars, and that he told her he was going to kill her and throw her baby out after he did so. Kathleen Johns Patterson Police Department Patterson, CA Patterson CA The Kathleen Johns Incident

Louis Myers arrested after traffic accident Suspect Event **2 Youths Arrested After Traffic Crash**

Two Vallejo youths were arrested by police shortly after midnight Thursday after their car plowed into a pickup parked in the 200 block of B Street.

Officers said a compact driven north on B Street by James W. Sturgess, 18, of 1207 Warren Ave., struck the parked truck registered to Bruce Smith, of 231 B St., inflicting moderate damage.

Police said Sturgess and his passenger, Louis J. Myers, 18, of 468 La Jolla St., were injured in the crash and were taken to Kaiser Hospital. Sturgess was treated for bruised hands and Myers was given first aid for multiple abrasions and bruises of the forehead and knee as well as cuts on the nose and above the left eye.

Officers said Myers was placed under arrest for being under the influence of drugs while Sturgess was booked at the branch county jail here on felony drunk driving. Louis (Louie) Joseph Myers Vallejo, CA Vallejo CA

items.title: Riverside connection is published in SF Chronicle
items.subtitle: Media Event • Mon, Nov 16, 1970
items.searchText:
Riverside connection is published in SF Chronicle Media Event **New Evidence in Zodiac Killings**
**A Link to Murder in Riverside**
_By Paul Avery, Chronicle Correspondent_

Riverside — Persuasive evidence turned up by _The Chronicle_ — that Zodiac murdered a beautiful Riverside college coed four years ago — may provide new and important clues leading to the capture of California’s mystery killer.

And a young San Bernardino woman, who eight months ago escaped a weird murder attempt by Zodiac, has given _The Chronicle_ additional details as to what he looks like.

A number of chilling similarities link the savage October 30, 1966, knife slaying of 18-year-old Cheri Jo Bates with Zodiac — whose most recent boast is that he has killed 14 persons.

Until now, police have doubted this claim. They had felt he actually killed five persons in attacks in San Francisco, at Lake Berryessa, and around Vallejo, which began with a lovers’ lane ambush murder of two teenagers in December 1968.


### **Messages**

Homicide detectives didn’t even tie together these crimes until mid-1969, when Zodiac began corresponding with _The Chronicle_, starting his macabre messages:
“This is the Zodiac speaking.”

At the time of Miss Bates’ murder, Zodiac had not yet proclaimed his infamous name. The killing, in spite of certain bizarre circumstances, until now has been considered the work of some rejected school suitor.

The murder happened this way:

Miss Bates, an honor student, drove her Volkswagen to Riverside City College early on a Sunday evening to draw some books from the school library. She spent 20 minutes selecting them, then returned to the car at 6:10 p.m. with two friends.

She started the ignition key, stepped on the gas pedal, and heard a coarse grinding noise as the engine wouldn’t catch.


### **Passenger**

Suddenly, a man appeared — apparently either a passing stranger or, as some have suspected, the man who had disabled her car.

Other suggested the killer had rigged a nearby streetlight to blink out, sending Miss Bates into darkness as she walked toward a parking lot.

She and the man talked briefly. Witnesses later said they appeared friendly. She seemed neither frightened nor annoyed.

Then she moved toward the alley beside the library and disappeared into the darkness.

Her body was found at 6:30 a.m. the next morning, sprawled face-down and slashed. Her throat had been cut. She had been stabbed multiple times.

Her lightweight clothing was torn in several places, suggesting a violent struggle. One of her shoes was missing.


### **Envelopes**

Two unstamped envelopes were found outside the Riverside Police Department and the Riverside Press-Enterprise newspaper office about a month later.

Each contained a printed greeting card and a handwritten note beginning, “BATES HAD TO DIE — THERE WILL BE MORE.”

The letters bore block-printed addresses similar to Zodiac’s later style, including a distinct slant to the left, crossed “7” figures, and peculiar “K” forms.

Handwriting experts compared them with Zodiac’s later letters and declared “unquestionable similarities.”

A third note, found scribbled on a desk at the Riverside City College Library, read in part:
“SHE WANTED TO DIE. THERE WILL BE MORE.”

Experts believe this may have been the first “confession” letter Zodiac ever wrote.


### **Details**

Police later discovered her car’s ignition coil wire had been pulled loose. Grease smears were found on her clothing, and a man’s Timex wristwatch with a torn band was recovered nearby.

Hair samples clutched in her hand showed she fought her killer fiercely.

There was no sexual molestation.

The killer left no fingerprints.


### **Conclusion**

For years, Riverside police insisted the case was not connected with Zodiac. Captain Cross of the homicide detail told _The Chronicle_ that they once suspected a fellow student who was later cleared.

But in October 1970, Zodiac sent a letter to _The Chronicle_ hinting that “there’s a murder some of your boys in Riverside can’t solve.”

Within days, police in Riverside and San Francisco compared files.

The typewriter used on the 1966 notes matched the type in Zodiac’s known correspondence.

The similarities in phrasing, handwriting, and method were too strong to ignore.

Captain Cross and San Francisco Homicide Inspector David Toschi agreed that the Bates murder now “must be seriously considered Zodiac’s first known killing.”


### **Car**

An even more recent connection comes from another woman — Mrs. Kathleen Johns, 23, who says Zodiac tried to murder her and her infant daughter near Modesto last March.

She told police she was lured into his car by an offer of help after her own had broken down. He drove aimlessly along country roads for more than an hour before she escaped with her child into a field.

The man, she said, had a round face, light hair, and a heavy voice with a slight drawl — closely matching composite sketches of Zodiac.


### **Identification**

Shown a police artist’s sketch of the cabdriver Paul Stine’s killer, Mrs. Johns said: “That’s him.”

Asked that night to describe the attack, she trembled and said, “My God — he said he was going to kill me and my baby.”

She later identified the man as Zodiac after reading of the murders in the papers.

She still receives threatening letters signed “Z.”

Her story was corroborated by several highway patrolmen who had earlier seen her car abandoned and the suspect’s vehicle cruising the area.


### **Epilogue**

The evidence now strongly indicates that Zodiac first killed in Riverside, continued through the Bay Area, and may still be active.

Police in both cities have exchanged complete files and are working jointly with the FBI to analyze latent fingerprints and the anonymous “Bates had to die” notes.

**Cheri Jo Bates — 1966 murder victim.**

Zodiac’s confirmed toll — including Bates — would then stand at six known victims, with eight additional possible cases under review. San Francisco Chronicle Paul Avery Cheri Jo Bates San Francisco The Murder of Cheri Jo Bates

items.title: Ralph Spinelli arrested for Portland armed robbery
items.subtitle: Witness Event • Thu, Feb 11, 1971
items.searchText:
Ralph Spinelli arrested for Portland armed robbery Witness Event **Crazy Horse Saloon Owner Held On Warrant**

**FAIRFIELD** — The owner of the Crazy Horse Saloon in rural Fairfield was arrested Thursday and booked in county jail on a governor’s extradition warrant.

Ralph A. Spinelli Jr., is charged with the June 8 armed robbery of Mary’s Club in Portland, Ore. Three men allegedly took $17,000 in the hold-up.

Superior Judge Raymond Sherwin denied a writ of habeas corpus filed yesterday.

Los Angeles attorney Harrison W. Hertzberg, who represents Spinelli, states that Spinelli has been denied his constitutional rights in violation of the fourth amendment (right of search and seizure regulated).

Judge Sherwin noted that the arraigning court is authorized to admit Spinelli to bail in the sum of $5,000.

An affidavit submitted in support of the seizure listed items confiscated from the Crazy Horse Saloon as a result of search in July and authorized by Fairfield municipal Judge Curtis A. Singleton.

Items found included a Lugar pistol, a .38 cal. snub nose revolver, a .45 cal. automatic, false sideburns, a false mustache, a salt and pepper colored man’s wig, and $17,000 in $100 denominations.

The Oregon police have stated that three people were involved in the robbery. One man was possibly salt and pepper in color.

The suspects used a .38 cal. snub nose revolver and a .45 cal. automatic. One suspect was wearing a false mustache and possibly false sideburns.

All four victims in the hold-up identified Spinelli in a line-up according to Oregon police.

Spinelli was arraigned before municipal Judge Wallace Cox in Vallejo. Ralph Spinelli Fairfield, CA Fairfield CA

items.searchText: Paul Doerr writes anti-smoking letter to editor Suspect Event \* \* WHAT ABOUT THE VICTIMS? To Harry MacArthur and his letter on smoking. You have the right to smoke, or take dope, or shoot yourself, but only where your actions won't inconvenience me. You write like many of the bleeding hearts who worry about the criminal's rights and forget about the victims. My right is to breathe clean air, not contaminated by your smoke and it is superior to your right to spread filth. PAUL DOERR Vallejo. Paul Alfred Doerr, Jr. Paul Alfred Doerr Jr. Vallejo, CA Vallejo CA

items.title: Ronald Allen hired by Santa Rosa, CA as landscape architect
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Sun, Oct 10, 1971
items.searchText:
Ronald Allen hired by Santa Rosa, CA as landscape architect Suspect Event **RECRUITMENT YEAR**
**Three New Officials Start Work for City**

The city of **Santa Rosa** has three new officials at work in planning, renewal, and personnel.

Filling the positions marks a year of active recruitment by the city to fill vacancies and find qualified employees for new posts.

Working in new positions are **Michael (Mike) Sappingfield**, 27, the city’s first personnel analyst; **James K. Burns**, 37, the urban renewal administrator; and **Ronald G. Allen**, 32, who assumes landscape architect duties as an associate planner.

Mr. Sappingfield works in the personnel office under Personnel Director Al Wiers. Mr. Burns manages renewal matters for Donald Laidlaw, city director of community development. Mr. Allen works in the planning department under Loren Matthias, assistant planning director.

Sappingfield, a native of Ukiah, comes to Santa Rosa from Sacramento, where he worked as a personnel analyst for four years. A 1967 graduate of **Sacramento State**, he is currently pursuing a master’s degree. He previously worked in that city’s recruitment and examination section, including minority recruitment in the affirmative action program. He and his wife, **Patty**, live at 547 Simpson St. with their daughter, Christina.

Mr. Burns, formerly of Rochester, N.Y., worked the past 15 months as deputy administrator for the Department of Urban Renewal and Economic Development, and as director of the Bureau of Redevelopment and Relocation, managing all municipal parking for Rochester. Before that, he was manager of the Bunker Hill urban renewal project in Los Angeles for two years and previously held positions with the Sacramento Redevelopment Agency.

A 1962 graduate of **Sacramento State** in industrial management, he spent five years in the Air Force and now holds the reserve rank of captain. He is a member of the National Association of Housing and Redevelopment Officials. His interests include training hunting dogs, hunting quail, and playing golf. Mr. Burns, his wife **Elizabeth**, and sons **Kenneth (14)**, **Darin (11)**, and **Scott (4)** live at 2245 Cummings Dr.

Mr. Allen, a **1971 graduate of the University of California, Berkeley**, is a native of **Vallejo**. He is currently working on the **open space element** of the Santa Rosa General Plan and reviewing landscaping plans. He is also called upon to design work for other city departments.

A 1968 graduate of **Cal Poly Pomona**, he also holds a master’s degree in **landscape architecture** from **UC Berkeley**. His interests include skin diving, fishing, and skiing. Mr. Allen and his wife, **Karen Marie**, live in Vallejo. Ronald Gene Allen Santa Rosa, CA Santa Rosa CA Arthur Leigh Allen

items.title: The Badlands Letter is mailed
items.subtitle: Disputed Z Communication • Wed, May 8, 1974
items.searchText:
The Badlands Letter is mailed Disputed Z Communication Postcard criticizing the film Badlands, signed “a citizen.” (Mailing is a fact; authorship disputed by some researchers.)

Sirs—I would like to express~~ion~~ my ~~consternt~~ consternation concerning your poor taste & lack of sympathy for the public, as evidenced by your running of the ads for the movie "Badlands," featuring the blurb—"In 1959 most people were killing time. Kit & Holly were killing people." In light of recent events, this kind of murder-glorification can only be deplorable at best (not that glorification of violence was ever justifiable) why don't you show some concern for public sensibilities & cut the ad?

A citizen Zodiac Oakland, CA San Francisco San Francisco Chronicle offices Oakland CA Zodiac Letters

items.title: Bryan Hartnell passes the bar
items.subtitle: Victim Event • Sat, Nov 29, 1975
items.searchText:
Bryan Hartnell passes the bar Victim Event Sixteen San Bernardino County residents were among 3,550 people who passed the fall bar examination, the State Bar Association has announced. In addition to passing the general bar examination, each applicant must satisfy other requirements before they can be admitted to practice law, including passing an examination of legal ethics, the state bar announced. About 92 per cent of the individuals listed have now satisfied all requirements, the bar said. The admittees will be sworn as attorneys at ceremonies in midDecember in Sacramento, San Diego, San Francisco, Los Angeles, Fresno, and San Bernardino.
The San Bernardino ceremonies will be held Dec. 19 at 10:30 a.m. in the State Building at 303 W. Third Street. The persons are: Laurel Anne Benson, Crestline; Leslie 0.
Bradbury, Claremont; Terry E. Caldwell, Victorville; James Gordon Davis, Ontario; Gloria Gebbie, San Bernardino; Benjamin Ygnacio Gonzales, San Bernardino; Bryan Calvin Hartnell, Redlands; Gerald M. Hinkley, San Bernardino; Christopher Joseph Miller, Ontario; George Stephen Roberts, Claremont; Stanley Glenn Rothbart, Ontario; Wilfrid W. H. Soelter, Upland; Alan Errol Spears, San Bernardino; Alan Dean Tuck Jr., Upland; Charles J. Uhalley, Cucamonga, and Mary Elizabeth Vincent, San Bernardino. The names and addresses are those released by the state Bar, which advised that it is sometimes provided with only a student's law school or other temporary address. Bryan Calvin Hartnell The San Bernardino County Sun San Bernardino, CA San Bernardino CA

items.title: Vallejo Times-Herald article on Toschi
items.subtitle: Case Event • Fri, Sep 10, 1976
items.searchText:
Vallejo Times-Herald article on Toschi Case Event _Here is a Gemini AI transcription of the article about Dave Toschi:_
### Lone SF Detective Is Chasing ‘Zodiac’
**By TIM REITERMAN**
SAN FRANCISCO (AP) — For seven years, Police Det. Dave Toschi has been looking for one particular pot-bellied man, about 5-feet-8 tall, 200 pounds, with short reddish brown hair and glasses on his round face. 1
The man appears intelligent and may have learned cryptography in the military. 2 He sometimes wears pleated pants over sine shoes. On weekdays, he may work at a menial job where he is looked upon as little more than a name and number. On weekends, he takes pleasure in killing unarmed strangers. 3
He calls himself Zodiac. 4
In boastful, taunting, sometimes coded communications, Zodiac has claimed a number of murders. 5
Zodiac is a personal nemesis for Toschi, now the only San Francisco Police Department officer assigned to the case. 6 His case files fill seven large drawers, one for each frustrating year on Zodiac’s trail. He carries a copy of Zodiac’s last letter at all times — a reminder that the killer may still be at large. 7
“To be honest, I’m no closer now to solving the case,” the dapper, curly-haired detective says with a tug at his bow tie. 8“It’s been a paper chase for me. My files are getting larger. I doubt I’ll get him unless he makes a mistake or kills again. I don’t know where he is.” 9
### UNSOLVED MURDERS
“He has a personal box score,” the homicide detective says. “We know for sure he killed at least six and wounded two in the late 1960s, but there are many unsolved homicides. He hasn’t taken credit for any specific killings in the last few years, yet whenever we get a new letter postmarked from him, the score goes up.” 10
Zodiac has claimed responsibility for a host of murders, several of which police say he definitely did not commit. 11But they are certain the following attacks belong in his “boxscore”: 12
- On Oct. 30, 1966, a man slashed the throat of a University of California coed, Cheri Jo Bates, in a Riverside parking lot. 13
- On Dec. 20, 1968, a man used a pencil flashlight taped to the barrel of a .22-caliber pistol to find and kill David Faraday and Betty Lou Jensen on a dark lover’s lane near Vallejo. 14
- On July 5, 1969, less than four miles from the double murder, a man repeatedly fired a 9mm pistol into another parked car, killing Mrs. Darlene Ferrin, 22, and wounding Michael Mageau, 19. 15
- A black-hooded man tied up and repeatedly stabbed Bryan Hartnell and Cecelia Ann Shepard at Lake Berryessa Park, about 20 miles north of Napa. The woman died from stab wounds but Hartnell recovered. 16161616
- On Oct. 11, 1969, a man pumped four bullets into the head of taxi driver Paul Stine, 29, in a posh San Francisco neighborhood. 17171717
### STRANGE PATTERN
The first known slaying and the last known slaying are considered particularly strange. Zodiac did not boast of the first, the only slaying outside the San Francisco Bay area. And the last did not fit his pattern. 18
“The other killings were in remote areas and no money was taken,” Toschi observed. “This last one was a sloppy holdup.” 19“He had to have gotten blood on his hands and clothing. Maybe he got a total of $8.” 20
Did Zodiac need money, or did he want to change his techniques, never using the same weapon twice? He concealed his face during a daylight murder, and he used a knife rather than a gun when shots would attract attention. 21212121
### HIS MOTIVATION
What drives Zodiac to kill? 22
In his own decoded words, Zodiac said, “I like killing people because it is so much fun, it is more fun than killing wild game in the forest because man is the most dangerous animal of all, to kill something gives me the most thrilling experie23nce. The best part of it is that when I die I will be reborn in paradice, and all I have killed will become my slaves.” 242424242424242424
Toschi tends to dismiss the witchcraft-Satanic implications. “He was trying to be dramatic. If he had much of an interest in the occult, he never would have misspelled paradise as ‘paradice.’ I think the letters are a screen, not a real clue.” 25
Is Zodiac a perverse sex killer? “There’s no evidence he ever molested any of his victims, nor did he do any fondling or use filthy language,” Toschi says. 26“Some psychiatrists will tell you he’s a latent homosexual or he hates women. I think he gets sexual gratification or pleasure from killing unarmed people. One victim said he was laughing loudly and hideously while stabbing a woman victim.” 2728
### SUSPICIOUS WIVES
Toschi says this after pinning down 2,000 possible suspects whose names were provided by concerned citizens, police officers, prison officials and others. 29
“I’ve even had wives come in and tell me they suspect their husband,” he says. 30“But I never joke about the Zodiac. I’ll talk to anybody about the case on the chance they know something.” 31
Zodiac has not sent a direct statement in months, but that’s not unprecedented. 32In January 1974, after a silence of 34 months, he wrote to update his murder count and to pan the gut-wrenching movie, “The Exorcist,” as “bad comedy.” 33Then in July 1974, he wrote a letter to the San Francisco Chronicle, decrying ads for the movie “Badlands” as “murder glorification.” 34“Why don’t you show some concern for public sensibilities and cut this ad,” printed the man who terrorized the area with grisly murders and occasional threats against busloads of children. 35353535The letter was signed, “A Citizen,” but alert mail room workers recognized his printing, which was then verified by handwriting experts. 36
“When we get intervals of silence, it’s possible he’s in the hospital or in prison where he wouldn’t dare send a letter,” Toschi says. “But this is only supposition.” 37
### FOUR QUESTIONS
A newspaper article will prompt a rash of tips. He has consulted psychics and astrologers for clues. Four men considered likely suspects were placed under surveillance, fingerprinted and questioned extensively. But nothing has panned out. 38
Just what do police know about the mystery killer? 39They have partial fingerprints but not enough to make an identification. 40They have an admittedly weak physical description based on accounts of survivors and witnesses. 41414141They are almost certain he wears glasses since he even wore them during the daytime stabbing at Lake Berryessa. 42They believe he lives in the San Francisco Bay area since most of his communications were mailed from this area. 43434343
Judging from Zodiac’s physical description, his use of dated jargon, Toschi estimates that he may be 35 to 45 years old. “I would speculate he maybe has a menial job, is not well thought of and blends into the crowd,” he said. “He might be a room clerk or work for a big insurance company. A name and a number.” 44444444
“In 1969, I felt we’d solve the case. Zodiac would make a mistake or he’d come in and surrender,” Toschi says. “I never thought then that I’d still be working on the case now. Dammit, I hope I can solve it. I have seven or eight years before I’m eligible for retirement.” 45454545 David Toschi Vallejo Times-Herald Vallejo, CA San Francisco Vallejo CA

items.title: Vallejo Police interview with Karen re: Andrew Todd Walker
items.subtitle: Case Event • Fri, Jun 10, 1977
items.searchText:
Vallejo Police interview with Karen re: Andrew Todd Walker Case Event On 6-10-77, contact was made with one Karen [REDACTED], WFA, 25 years, [REDACTED] phone [REDACTED]. Reporting Officer requested an interview with Miss [REDACTED] in regard to the above mentioned incident with Darlene Ferrin. She complied. At VPD Miss [REDACTED] related that she was seventeen years old and baby-

\* \*\*SIGNATURE OF REPORTING OFFICER:\*\* Lieutenant Husted
\* \*\*ID NUMBER:\*\* 253

---
\### \*\*Page 4\*\*

\*\*AGENCY:\*\* Vallejo Police
\*\*CASE NUMBER:\*\* 243 164
\*\*PAGE NO:\*\* 4

\*\*NARRATIVE\*\*

sitting for Dean and Darlene Ferrin. Again, reiterated the months were from January through May of 1969. Stated she quit in May to overcome some guilt that she was feeling about covering up for Darlene's running around with other men while she was married to Dean. One of the men mentioned was the surviving victim in the Vallejo homicide Michael Mageau.

Karen relates one incident that allegedly occurred while she was babysitting in approximately February or March 1969 at 560 Wallace. She stated she was babysitting approximately 10:00 p.m. one night, was looking out the front window of the Ferrin's apartment, and she noticed a man whom she took to be middleaged sitting in a white American-made sedan watching the apartment. She stated he was there for several hours but left before Dean returned from work shortly after midnight. She stated she could not really describe this individual and only knew he was middleaged because he either turned on the inside car light or lit a cigarette so that she got a partial look at the man. She did not note the license number and was only certain of the approximate age - middleaged, and the fact that the sedan was white.

The following day she related this incident to Darlene Ferrin who at the time recalled was in the bathroom putting on her makeup to go out. Darlene asked her what the car and the man looked like, and after relating a description to her, Dee said something to the effect, "I guess he's checking up on me again. I heard he was back from out of state." Followed by, "He doesn't want anyone to know what I (meaning Darlene) saw him do. I saw him murder someone."

Karen stated that Dee sometime during this conversation mentioned the man's name, and that the first name was very short, three or four letters and that the second name was just slightly longer. The name was quite common. Karen stated that Dee appeared to be genuinely frightened of this individual.

\* \*\*SIGNATURE OF REPORTING OFFICER:\*\* Lieutenant Husted
\* \*\*ID NUMBER:\*\* 263

---
\### \*\*Page 5\*\*

\*\*AGENCY:\*\* Vallejo Police
\*\*CASE NUMBER:\*\* 243 164
\*\*PAGE NO:\*\* 5

\*\*NARRATIVE\*\*

and mentioned sometime that he had been checking up on her at Terry's Restaurant where Darlene was employed.

At the conclusion of her statement, Reporting Officer discussed with Karen the possibility of her recalling exactly what the name might have been, and a better description of the individual if she were to undergo hypnosis. Stated that she never had any experience with this, but that she would be willing to submit to anything that may assist in the investigation. At this time Reporting Officer advised Karen that arrangements would be made for a session with a hypnotist to see if we could produce more information, and that Reporting Officer would be in contact.

On 6-15-77, Reporting Officer contacted Lieutenant Larry Haynes, Concord Police Department, phone 415-671-3030, in regards to the hypnotic session. Lieutenant Haynes has been trained in this by the Law Enforcement Hypnotic Institute in Los Angeles Police Department and has had much experience in this area. When asked as to the feasibility of such an examination with an 8-year time span, Lieutenant Haynes replied much success has been had regardless of the number of years that has passed. Advised Lieutenant Haynes that the interview primarily would focus on Karen's ability to recall a name told to her by the Zodiac victim Darlene Ferrin and possibly focus on a painting party that occurred at Darlene Ferrin's residence where possibly the alleged subject was in attendance. Lieutenant Haynes agreed to assist. An appointment was made for 6-16-77 at 1300 hours. Reporting Officer then re-contacted Karen [REDACTED], advised her of the appointment set for 1300 hours. She agreed to meet Reporting Officer at the Vallejo Police Department. William Joseph Grant Karen (Ellendy?)  William Joseph Grant, aka Andrew Todd Walker

items.title: The 1978 “I am back with you” Letter
items.subtitle: Disputed Z Communication • Mon, Apr 24, 1978
items.searchText:
The 1978 “I am back with you” Letter Disputed Z Communication A 1978 “return” letter was mailed to the San Francisco Chronicle. Its authenticity has been  disputed by experts over the years, beginning not long after publication when columnist Armistead Maupin reported that Inspector Dave Toschi had seemingly sent fan mail about himself to the writer.

**_Transcription_**
Dear Editor
This is the Zodiac speaking I am back with you. Tell herb caen I am here, I have always been here. That city pig toschi is good - but I am bu smarter and better he will get tired then leave me alone. I am waiting for a good movie about me. who will play me. I am now in control of all things.
Yours truly:
⌖ - guess
SFPD - 0 Zodiac David Toschi San Francisco Chronicle offices Zodiac Letters

items.title: Joan Webster's suitcase found in Greyhound Bus Station
items.subtitle: Case Event • Fri, Jan 29, 1982
items.searchText:
Joan Webster's suitcase found in Greyhound Bus Station Case Event Via justiceforjoanwebster.com
> Joan's Lark suitcase was discovered at the Park Square Greyhound Bus Station.
> 
> The location was about five miles west of Logan Airport.
> 
> The suitcase was placed in locker #1434 where it remained for 30 days. It was placed there before 9:30 a.m. on November 29, 1981.
> 
> The suitcase was removed from the locker after 30 days and was stored in a caged room for another 30 days before an employee noticed the tag and called police.
> 
> Key #3711 for locker #1434 was not recovered.
> 
> The FBI catalogued the contents and reported the bag was undisturbed. Joan Webster Boston, MA Boston MA
