items.title: Ralph Spinelli claims he knows who Zodiac was
items.subtitle: Case Event • Fri, Dec 14, 1990
items.searchText:
Ralph Spinelli claims he knows who Zodiac was Case Event From Zodiac Unmasked:

**Friday, December 14, 1990**

Ralph Spinelli, lying in a cell on his fiftieth birthday, sweated into the thin mattress. Jim Overstreet, San Jose P.D., had arrested him as a suspect in at least nine armed robberies of restaurants. Spinelli knew he could be prosecuted as a career criminal. He had been arrested in Oregon in 1972 for a series of armed robberies of restaurants and served two years of a ten-year sentence for them. Facing a thirty-year sentence, he suggested to Overstreet that he had something to trade. In jail, cigarettes, sex, and secrets were three prized commodities. “The best kind of informant is a two-time loser-forty-five to fifty who knows if he goes up one more time he’s going to die in the can,” said a detective. “When he turns to us, he has nowhere else to go—he becomes as zealous as a cop.” The cops had a “twist,” a hammer, on Spinelli and he was “working his beef.”

“I know the real name of Zodiac,” Spinelli said finally. “It was his ace.”

Spinelli began to talk in halting tones and rapid cadence. But for all he said, it was just enough to get Overstreet on the hook. Overstreet realized Spinelli might be inventing a story—a very good chance of that. However, he could not ignore what might be a valid tip. Zodiac was the biggest case there was. He left a message for retired Detective Bawart and Bawart rang him right back. “Spinelli is currently incarcerated in the Santa Clara County jail,” advised Overstreet. “He won’t divulge what information he has regarding Zodiac unless some kind of deal is made regarding the present charges against him.” Bawart sighed. He had heard such claims about Zodiac before. “I’ll get back to you,” he replied, and called Conway.

“There was this guy Spinelli living in San Jose,” Bawart told me later, “pulled a bunch of stickups and they caught him and he said, ‘Get ahold of Vallejo P.D. I’ll tell you all about a case they’re real interested in.’ They called us, and Conway and I went down and interviewed him on his birthday.” Vallejo Police Captain Roy Conway already knew Spinelli. His name had been prominent in the Vallejo area during the forties, fifties, and sixties. Connections with organized crime were suspected.

Conway, a Vallejo police officer since 1965, had worked with Mulanax, since retired. He was presently commander of the Investigation Division, but the night Ferrin was murdered and Mageau terribly wounded, he had been a sergeant and he and Richard Hoffman had been the first policemen at the scene. Conway personally verified the authenticity of descriptions Zodiac mailed to the press back in 1969. He believed Zodiac had committed those shootings. An anonymous phone call to the Vallejo P.D. had been made from a phone booth near Arthur Leigh Allen’s home and near where Tucker kept his car.

Conway and Bawart drove to the Santa Clara lockup, checked their guns, and went in to see Spinelli. He was being held on $500,000 bail while awaiting trial on the charges. The prisoner was anxious. Spinelli knew how much trouble he was in. Though he could go in for what amounted to life, Spinelli still refused to budge. He would not name the individual he knew as Zodiac without being offered a deal. “I want all charges against me dropped,” he insisted.

“Out of the question,” said Bawart.

“We refuse to make any sort of deal like that,” said Conway.

“And just how do you know this person is Zodiac?” asked Bawart.

“He threatened me several days before the killing of Paul Stine, the San Francisco cabdriver. He wanted to show me how tough he was. He told me he was going to San Francisco and kill a cabbie for me,” said Spinelli. “The next day or so a cabdriver was killed in San Francisco and the Zodiac took credit for it.” A police report confirmed Spinelli and Allen had had fights in the past.

After a long discussion, Spinelli agreed to give Zodiac’s true name to his lawyer, Craig Kennedy, a deputy public defender of Santa Clara County. Throughout the remainder of the month, several meetings were held between Kennedy and his immediate boss, Bryan Schechmeister. He refused to agree that no further information would be divulged without a definite deal given to their client. Thus Conway and Bawart still refused to do. They could play hardball too. It would be interesting to see who blinked first.


**Thursday, January 31, 1991**
**Page 2**

Conway received a call from Kennedy. “I can give you the name that Spinelli has,” he said. The other side had caved.

Conway waited silently, although he already thought he knew the answer.

“It’s Leigh Allen. Arthur Leigh Allen.”

Conway let out a slow breath. He already knew all about Lee Allen. He got Bawart on the phone immediately. “LEE” was in the Zodiac cipher. It was the name Allen had worn on his smock at Ace Hardware. Conway contacted Inspector Armstrong in San Francisco. “Yes,” Armstrong said, “one of the primary suspects back in the early 1970s was Arthur Leigh Allen.” He also verified that Allen’s name had never been published in any media to his knowledge. The only persons knowing his name were law enforcement people and only those involved in the actual Zodiac investigation, or someone Allen might have bragged to such as medical personnel at Atascadero and some coworkers at an auto parts store in Sonoma.

“Arthur Leigh Allen and Spinelli were acquaintances way back when,” Bawart told me later, “and he [Allen] approached him and told him he wanted to be an enforcer for him. The guy [Spinelli] owned a topless bar. He admitted he was the Zodiac, said Spinelli, and said, ‘To show you, I’m going to go to San Francisco and kill a guy.’ That was shortly before the Stine killing. Then he supposedly came back and said, ‘I’m responsible for the Stine killing.’ I know this guy Spinelli fairly well from his criminal past. I didn’t know whether to believe him or not.”

Spinelli might have invented his story and been trying to win favor. Even if what he said was true, it was not solid supporting evidence that would hold up in court. It was fruit from a tainted tree. But how had Spinelli known to come up with the name Leigh Allen in the first place?

**Wednesday, February 6, 1991**

Conway and Bawart drove to the State Department of Justice, Sacramento Homicide Unit, to see Agent Fred Shirisago. This branch was currently the repository for all the Zodiac cases in the multitude of jurisdictions where the killer had struck. A constant refrain from amateur sleuths regarding the state DOJ was that some at the top level were not always responsive to their information and theories. “They don’t always take my information seriously,” one told me. “Further one guy claimed that the Riverside killing invalidated my material and he had proof Zodiac did not kill Cheri Jo Bates. He claimed the information provided by Pam, Darlene’s sister, was not valid as it was too old. The handprinting I provided by my expert was that ‘it looked similar,’ but after a killing in Martinez, they said it was not Zodiac’s. Yet he refused to allow the other expert, Mr. Morrill, a chance to examine said handprinting.”

This was not Toschi’s approach. “Just let people know you appreciate getting their clues on how to catch Zodiac,” he told me. “I always acknowledge receipt of a person’s letter, especially if it appears to have some substance or is sincere. I never know when I’ll get a letter that will make or break the case.” Shirisago gave Conway all the reports pertaining to the investigation of Arthur Leigh Allen, records that demonstrated San Francisco detectives, specifically Armstrong and Toschi, had conducted the primary investigation on Allen. They spoke with Armstrong, then Conway contacted Mel Nicolai, now retired. Nicolai agreed with Armstrong that the media had never revealed the name of Lee Allen as a suspect in the Zodiac investigation. Bawart followed the local papers devotedly. He too knew Allen’s name had never been linked publicly with Zodiac. Spinelli had to have had personal contact with Lee Allen or with a close friend of his. In no other way could he have come up with the name.

“Were there any reasons for Spinelli to lie about the information?” I asked Bawart. “A grudge? A lighter sentence of some sort?”

“Allen had gotten in a fight with Spinelli,” he told me. “Showed up at his house and kicked in the door and beat him up. And yes, he provided the information in exchange for a lighter sentence.”

Through Sacramento, Conway requested the FBI Director’s aid, Teletyping the following:

“The latent fingerprint section is requested to provide Sacramento with photographs of all latent fingerprints developed during the Zodiac investigation, Latent Case No A-10042. The latent fingerprint section is further requested to search the latent fingerprints developed in the Zodiac case through the Automated Identification System and the Automated Latent Systems Model and National Unidentified Latent File. Sacramento is not aware of the requested search as previously being conducted. The photographs of latent prints on file with the Identification Division are being sought at the request of the Vallejo California Police Department, who intends to run the prints, search through the California Department of Justice Automated Latent Prints System [ALPS].”

Compared against every suspect in the Zodiac case, prisoners and military personnel, there was no match. How had Zodiac done it?


**Thursday, February 7, 1991**

Bawart contacted Larry Ankron, a psychologist for VICAP at Quantico. Ankron knew all about Zodiac. Bawart told him all Armstrong, Toschi, and Mulanax had learned back in 1971, then gave him the information that Spinelli had provided—the name of Arthur Leigh Allen. The bureau, as it turned out, already had a file (their largest single file in the Zodiac case) on this individual. In Ankron’s estimation, Zodiac got his much of his fun from taunting the police and reliving his murders through souvenirs he kept as from the violent acts themselves. “My studies,” he said, “show that persons who commit these types of crimes many times keep souvenirs or trophies from these criminal acts. They will take some type of article from their victims such as identification, pieces of clothing, and so on. This is so they can keep these in a hidden place and relive the incident many times over. They will keep journals and newspaper clippings of the crimes themselves. Those who keep these souvenirs have ingenious hiding places within their residences such as false walls, hidden safes, and so on. Many times these individuals will have a storage place at another location where they keep their souvenirs.”

Possibly Zodiac’s trophies were underwater at a site such as Lake Berryessa or in the walls of a trailer. Ankron believed the killings were still going on, or had gone on until recently. After all, Zodiac said he wasn’t going to write anymore and would make his killings appear as accidents. “The only reason I can find that the murders had stopped,” he said, “is that the man responsible had moved away—although the Zodiac crimes committed elsewhere would be readily identifiable because of his well-known M.O.]. He might have died. Or the police had come very close to apprehending him.” Many who had seen Zodiac or been part of Leigh Allen’s alibi had died mysteriously. A caretaker’s daughter saw Zodiac speeding away from Blue Rock Springs, and died several years after in a car accident. A landlady who could have provided Allen with an alibi perished from a heart attack. Mr. William White, a neighbor [whose son joined the Vallejo] force, had seen Allen return home on the day of the Lake Berryessa murder, and died shortly afterward. Frank Gasser, the raccoon hunter who had peered in the white Impala on Lake Herman Road the night of the murders, perished in a freak accident, dying suddenly two years later when he struck his head on a coffee table.

“You know,” Bawart told me, “when you investigate so many cases you get to be jaded to the degree that you have so many coincidences you just don’t believe in coincidences after a while. Where there’s smoke there’s fire.” He visited Ace Hardware, where Allen had once worked, and contacted Leigh’s coworker George Hieb. An ex-city employee, Hieb had worked at the Corporation Yard for many years. In his retirement he was working at Ace Hardware.

“Do you know Arthur Leigh Allen?” asked Bawart.

“I know him quite well,” said Hieb. “I visit his home almost on a weekly basis.” Though Leigh’s mother had died, he still kept his basement bedroom, though he could have control of the entire house. Hieb too had seen Allen’s guns. “A number of revolvers—I think they’re .22-caliber, and at least one semiautomatic pistol, but I don’t know the caliber.” Because Allen had been arrested by the Sonoma County Sheriff’s Department for molesting a small boy, the suspect was a felon. Consequently, he was not allowed by law to own any of the weapons Hieb had seen. But in the decade that Hieb had known Allen, he had never discussed the Zodiac case with him. That was puzzling. Leigh had discussed it with others, even led people to think in that particular direction. Bawart cautioned Hieb not to mention the visit, then re-interviewed a number of people who had information about Allen. As much current information about the suspect as possible had to be gathered before they confronted him.

Near the detached garage on the northeast side of 32 Fresno Street sat a broken-down blue “General Motors-type” auto. According to Hieb’s information, Allen owned an older black G.M. vehicle. In the driveway were an older-model white Mercedes-Benz and light-blue Volkswagen Karmann Ghia. Allen possessed a white pickup, and Darlene Ferrin had been followed by an American-made white sedan. A silver or ice-blue ’66 Chevy with California plates had been seen at Lake Berryessa the day of the attack.

To get to the lay of the land, Bawart drove over to 1545 Broadway, where Allen kept a boat and trailer. The location was a single-story residence on the west side of Broadway. To the north of the building stood a detached double-car garage packed with household goods. There were storage areas—places where Allen might have concealed weapons—a large lean-to garage, forty feet long and twenty feet deep, and a second storage area at the rear. George got out. Behind the garage, wind whipped grass in a vacant field. The scent of the Bay was in the air. In the center of the field stretched a twenty-five-foot-long blue and white sailboat on a trailer, partially draped by a blue tarp. Bawart mysteriously lifted the tarp and saw a California state number, NE2375Z. The boat had its small outboard motor on board, which would allow a person to stay at sea for many days at a time. Bawart also observed an open sailboat some ten feet long. “It looked more like a rowboat,” he said. Bawart jotted into his license notebook—09127FB. In writing or registration letters 32 F were possible, so could be 39127FB. He phoned his assistant, his sister-in-law Heidi said, “Allen’s been unemployed and living on the government for three years,” Conway had told him. “He goes diving at his place—likes Cat sailboats.” Bawart decided that in addition to a search similar to Fresno, the police should at least search the sailboat. But the evidence they sought might be sunken somewhere off the coast, beneath blue lake waters, or under the turgid waters of the Delta. Arthur Leigh Allen George Bawart Ralph Spinelli San Jose, CA Santa Clara County Jail San Jose CA Arthur Leigh Allen

items.title: Michael Mageau identifies Arthur Leigh Allen
items.subtitle: Case Event • Tue, Sep 17, 1991
items.searchText:
Michael Mageau identifies Arthur Leigh Allen Case Event ### **PAGE 1 OF 5**

**VALLEJO POLICE DEPARTMENT**
**NARRATIVE CONTINUATION**
Case No. 243146

**Reference:**
Michael Renault MAGEAU
WM, 102949
[Address redacted]

**081691**

Pursuant to a previous telephone conversation with Michael MAGEAU, I made arrangements with him to meet at the Ontario Airport in San Bernardino County. These arrangements were made for him and I to meet in front of the security office in this airport at 10:30 a.m. on 081691. I flew to Ontario Airport, arriving shortly after 10:00 a.m. I met MAGEAU promptly at 10:30 a.m. in front of the security office. MAGEAU was accompanied by an older white female that I would estimate to be in early 60’s. MAGEAU explained that he lived with this female and her husband in the residence in [redacted].

It should be noted at this time that MAGEAU was the surviving person in the Zodiac Killing which occurred on 070469. I briefly asked MAGEAU about the incident. MAGEAU indicated that he did, in fact, get a view of the responsible’s face. It was a very short duration of time as the responsible had shined a flashlight into his face and almost immediately began shooting him. He then switched the flashlight over to the area where the other person in the vehicle with him, Darlene FERRIN, was seated, and began shooting her. MAGEAU indicated that it was during this span of time that he was able to see the individual who had shot him.



### **PAGE 2 OF 5**

I advised MAGEAU that I had a group of photographs which I wished to show him, and that the responsible who shot him on July 4, 1969 may or may not be amongst these photographs, and merely because I was showing him pictures, he did not have to identify anyone. MAGEAU indicated he understood this.

I showed MAGEAU a photographic lineup containing six photographs. The fourth photograph to the right in this lineup is that of Arthur Leigh ALLEN. MAGEAU viewed this lineup for approximately 15 or 20 seconds. He then placed his finger on the photograph of Arthur Leigh ALLEN stating, “That’s him.” I asked MAGEAU how sure he was, and he indicated he was pretty sure; that the responsible had a round face like that, and then he pointed to the picture on the right of another individual. The picture he was now pointing to was that of a person whom I could only read the first [redacted]. The lineup is made up primarily of soundex driver’s licenses which have been stapled together, and I do not want to further remove the staples from this lineup at this time to more positively identify this individual.

I asked MAGEAU if he was also identifying this photograph. He emphatically stated “No,” just that the picture also had a round face like that of the picture that he had identified of ALLEN. I asked MAGEAU on a scale of 1–10 how sure he was of his identification of the photograph of ALLEN. MAGEAU stated, “It’s at least an 8. I’m very sure that this is the individual who shot me on July 4, 1969.”



### **PAGE 3 OF 5**

I then asked MAGEAU if he was shown any other photographs during the investigation of this case. MAGEAU indicated that shortly after the shooting, he was shown a number of photographs which he was unable to identify. He indicates that today is the first time he was shown a photograph wherein he was able to identify a responsible.

MAGEAU also indicated that 8–10 years ago when he was living in the City of Oxnard he was shown some photographs by the Oxnard Police Department and was unable to make an identification at that time. It should be noted that I was responsible for having Oxnard Police Department show a group of photographs to MAGEAU of a suspect that had been developed by the Department of Justice.

MAGEAU was asked if he had heard any of the recent publicity regarding the Zodiac Case. MAGEAU indicated that he had read in a newspaper that a suspect who was a disabled school teacher had been identified as a possible responsible in the Zodiac killings. MAGEAU indicated there was no picture with this article, and he has seen no TV coverage regarding the Zodiac Case recently.

MAGEAU indicates he has never seen the face of the individual he had identified today with the exception on July 4, 1969.



### **PAGE 4 OF 5**

MAGEAU was then asked if he had ever attended in 1969, a painting party at Darlene FERRIN’S residence. MAGEAU indicated that he had not, but he had heard about this party. He was asked if he knew the name of Ron ALLEN. MAGEAU indicated that name sounded familiar, but he can’t remember for sure. He was then asked if he knew the name of Leigh ALLEN. MAGEAU responded, “I don’t think so.”

MAGEAU was then asked about a lengthy rap sheet which I had obtained from the Department of Justice on him; wherein, he had used approximately 33 aliases. MAGEAU viewed this listed aliases and indicated that he had, in fact, used most of those names. MAGEAU indicated that he had previously been in quite a bit of trouble; that he was now a Born Again Christian and that he attended the First Assembly of God Church as well as the First Baptist Church. And, in fact, the woman with him and her husband were both church members in both of these churches, and they all attended. He was presently residing with this woman and her husband and doing handyman work for them. My impression of MAGEAU at this time that he is sincere in his religious beliefs and has put his lawless past behind him.



### **PAGE 5 OF 5**

MAGEAU indicated that he would cooperate with this investigation in any way possible. I indicated to MAGEAU that it may be necessary to fly him up to Northern California for a more in-depth interview. He indicated that he would have no objection to doing this. An appointment will be made with MAGEAU for a more in-depth interview.


**REPORTING OFFICER:** G. BAWART
**DATE:** 081991
**APPROVAL:** FC
**CASE NO.:** 243146
**VPD FORM 1002A** George Bawart Arthur Leigh Allen Ontario International Airport (San Bernardino) Attack at Blue Rock Springs Park

items.title: William Beeman Sr. accuses brother Jack
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Fri, Nov 1, 1991
items.searchText:
William Beeman Sr. accuses brother Jack Suspect Event **Lawyer says: ‘My brother was the Zodiac’ slayer**
_Vallejo man declares he is ‘absolutely certain’ his dead sibling was notorious killer_
_Examiner Staff Report_

VALLEJO — A highly respected Vallejo attorney says he is “absolutely certain” his deceased brother was the notorious and boastful Zodiac killer who murdered at least six people in California in the late 1960s.

William Beeman Sr., 70, who has practiced law for 37 years, accused his elder brother, Jack Steadman Beeman — who he said died of emphysema in Arizona in 1984 — during a news conference he held Thursday morning at the Solano County fairgrounds.

Beeman told a throng of reporters he would reveal the killer’s identity only after explaining how he had come to solve the mystery.
“I will just refer to him as ‘Jack the Zodiac,’” Beeman said.

Beeman then listed some reasons why he believed “Jack” was the Zodiac. These included such things as Jack’s shoe size, his alleged hatred of women, the fact that he’d worn a Timex watch and every month read the latest issue of _True Detective_ magazine, his habit of always misspelling common words, and that he was a devotee of astrology.

But witnesses also described the Zodiac as being about 5 foot 9 and 145 pounds. Jack Beeman, who repaired televisions and other electric appliances, was over 6 feet tall and more than 200 pounds.

William Beeman conceded his belief is based only on circumstantial evidence he began compiling in 1990 after reading _Zodiac,_ by San Francisco author Robert Graysmith.

And Beeman denied that the news conference had anything to do with his recently self-published book titled _Jack._

Beeman said he had not taken his “evidence” to any investigators in Solano County, where three of Zodiac’s murders were committed.

While short on conclusions, the event was long on drama. The sister of one of the Zodiac’s victims peered at a picture of Jack Beeman in the book before exclaiming that he looked like a man who once fixed the family’s television set.

“I think there may be something here, but I just don’t know,” said Pam Huckabay, whose sister, Darlene Ferrin, was killed in Vallejo in 1969. “I just want the man who killed my sister brought to justice. I don’t want another book, or another movie.”

The Zodiac killings in Northern California began in December 1968 when two high school students were fatally gunned down while parked at a lovers lane.

After the killings, Zodiac would write to newspapers, bragging about his crimes and boasting he would never be caught.

The last known letter from the Zodiac came in 1974. He has not been heard from since. Pamela Jean Suennen Huckaby William Langley Beeman Jack Steadman Beeman Vallejo, CA Vallejo CA

items.title: Arthur Leigh Allen dies
items.subtitle: Suspect Event • Wed, Aug 26, 1992
items.searchText:
Arthur Leigh Allen dies Suspect Event **Transcription:**

**Man Once Suspected In Zodiac Case Dies**
By Erin Hallissy
_Chronicle East Bay Bureau_

A man once suspected of being the Zodiac killer, who taunted police and terrorized Northern California with threats of murder, died Wednesday in Vallejo of a heart attack at the age of 58.

In 1971, attention focused on Arthur Leigh Allen when relatives and friends told police that he was acting erratically and that they feared he might be the sadistic slayer who killed at least six people and perhaps as many as 37 between 1966 and 1974.

In his book _Zodiac_, former _Chronicle_ editorial cartoonist Robert Graysmith described Allen during that time as “the strongest choice of most detectives” for the Zodiac killer. Graysmith could not be reached for comment yesterday.

Former San Francisco police detective Dave Toschi said yesterday that Allen was investigated as a suspect in 1971 and his trailer was searched, but there was never enough evidence to file charges against him or any other suspect.

Allen had reportedly told some people that he was the Zodiac killer.

“Mr. Allen was a very, very good suspect,” Toschi said. “We looked into Mr. Allen very closely.”

In the mid-1970s, Allen was convicted of child-molesting charges and sentenced to prison, said Vallejo Police Captain Roy Conway.

Last year, the Vallejo Police Department searched Allen’s home in Vallejo as part of a follow-up investigation into the unsolved case.

“We found some writings, some pipe bombs, some illegal weapons,” Conway said. “None of it was conclusive.”

Conway said Allen, who for many years was unemployed and lived on general assistance, cooperated with police. The officer described Allen as highly intelligent. His interests included diving and Hobie Cat sailboat racing.

Conway said there have been as many as 1,000 suspects in the Zodiac killings.

The killer who called himself “The Zodiac” sent chilling letters with coded clues to newspapers and detectives about a string of murders, which occurred in Vallejo, Lake Berryessa, San Francisco and Riverside.

“It was like he was taunting us and saying, ‘I’m better than you and smarter than you,’” Toschi said. “He actually set out a challenge and said, ‘Catch me if you can.’”

Last year, a Vallejo lawyer held a press conference to announce that his dead brother had been the Zodiac killer. William L. Beeman privately published a two-volume set called _Jack the Zodiac_ in which he wrote that 101 pieces of circumstantial evidence pointed to his brother, Jack Beeman. Arthur Leigh Allen Vallejo, CA Vallejo CA Arthur Leigh Allen

items.title: San Francisco Chronicle
items.subtitle: Media,Organization,Key Figure
items.searchText:
San Francisco Chronicle Media,Organization,Key Figure The San Francisco Chronicle is an American daily newspaper serving the San Francisco Bay Area. It traces its roots to January 16, 1865, when teenage brothers Charles and M. H. de Young founded the paper as the Daily Dramatic Chronicle. It adopted its current name in 1869 and, as San Francisco grew, the Chronicle became one of the largest newspapers on the West Coast by the 1880s ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle#:~:text=The%20San%20Francisco%20Chronicle%20is,the%20%2085%20by%201880)). Now owned by Hearst Communications, the broadsheet remains the dominant daily newspaper in San Francisco.
The Chronicle is inextricably linked to the Zodiac case because the killer used the newspaper as his main conduit to the public. On July 31 1969, a letter arrived at the Chronicle in which a person claimed responsibility for the Lake Herman Road and Blue Rock Springs murders and enclosed a portion of a cipher; the other two parts were mailed to the Vallejo Times‑Herald and the San Francisco Examiner. Over the next several years the killer sent a string of taunting letters, cards and cryptograms to newspapers — 17 of the 22 known communications were delivered to the Chronicle ([www.sfchronicle.com](https://www.sfchronicle.com/nation/article/zodiac-killer-case-how-the-san-francisco-13464935.php#:~:text=Zodiac%20Letter%20The%20Chronicle%20One,Zodiac%20Killer)). Among them were a letter containing part of taxi driver Paul Stine’s shirt, and a Halloween card that threatened Chronicle reporter Paul Avery. The Chronicle published many of these communications and played a central role in reporting on the case, but there is no evidence that anyone at the paper had any connection to the crimes; its involvement was as a journalistic intermediary between the killer and the public. items.title: Northern California Renaissance Pleasure Faire
items.subtitle: Pop Culture
items.searchText:
Northern California Renaissance Pleasure Faire Pop Culture <https://marinmagazine.com/community/history/history-of-the-renaissance-pleasure-faire/>
IF YOU CAME of age in California in the 1960s and were fortunate in your life choices, you no doubt at one time or another experienced the [Renaissance Pleasure Faire](http://renfair.com/socal/). Although it originated in 1963 in Southern California’s Agoura Hills (over 3,000 people attended), the [Northern California Renaissance Pleasure Faire](https://www.norcalrenfaire.com/) also had a memorable run. That one started at [Marin’s China Camp State Park](http://www.parks.ca.gov/?page_id=466) in 1967 and by 1970 had moved to Novato’s hilly Black Point area, where the above photo was taken.
Of course, the fair was headlined as taking place at Black Pointe Forest, for more authentic 16th-century English country flavor. Fair personnel, all 2,500 of them on average, attended 30 different classes and workshops in Elizabethan style and folklore and, once the fair began, wore period attire, including ruffs, jerkins and snoods. Booths in pastoral settings sold handicrafts of the bygone era, madrigal singers entertained and food stands offered fattened turkey legs and tankards of dark ale.
The Renaissance Pleasure Faire was born in the minds of Ron and Phyllis Patterson, an art director and art teacher, respectively; both were adamant that Renaissance history and customs be presented as accurately as possible. The couple lived for many years in Marin and divorced in 1980, and their brainchild was sold in 1994; today, imitator renaissance fairs are held throughout the world. Ron died at age 80 in 2011 while living in a Sausalito floating home; Phyllis, who lived in a log cabin in Novato, passed away in 2014 at 82. Their son Kevin, who grew up during the Renaissance Pleasure Faire’s most successful years and later was active in the business, still lives in Marin. He and his family manage [San Francisco’s annual Dickens Christmas Fair.](https://dickensfair.com/mobile/) Northern California Renaissance Pleasure Faire 3rd Annual Renaissance Pleasure Faire 1st Annual Renaissance Pleasure Fair in San Rafael 2nd Annual Renaissance Pleasure Faire 4th Annual Renaissance Pleasure Faire 9/27 Renaissance Pleasure Faire opens China Camp (Renaissance Pleasure Faire Site) San Rafael, CA Marin County, CA California SF Bay Area United States of America Renaissance Fairs

items.title: Pacific Union College
items.subtitle: Organization
items.searchText:
Pacific Union College Organization **Pacific Union College** (**PUC**) is a [private](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Private_university) [Seventh-day Adventist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church) [liberal arts college](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liberal_arts_colleges_in_the_United_States) in [Angwin, California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angwin,_California). It is the only four-year college in [Napa County](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napa_County,_California), and the twelfth oldest institution of higher education in California.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5) As a [coeducational](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coeducation) residential college with an almost exclusively undergraduate student body, most of those who attend the college are four-year students living on campus.
PUC is accredited by the [WASC Senior College and University Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WASC_Senior_College_and_University_Commission) and maintains various programmatic accreditation for specific programs. Enrollment at Pacific Union College is roughly 825.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:6-6) The college offers about 60 [undergraduate majors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Undergraduate_degree) and three [master's programs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Master%27s_degree) organized in 25 academic departments, with its [health science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Health_science) degrees the largest number of those sought out by students.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:3-7)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:6-6) The campus occupies 200 acres (0.81 km2) of the college's 1,800 acres (7.3 km2) in property.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:3-7)
History
[Sidney Brownsberger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sidney_Brownsberger), PUC's first president**Early years (1882–1908)**
Pacific Union College was founded as **Healdsburg Academy** in [Healdsburg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Healdsburg,_California), [California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California), in northern [Sonoma County](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sonoma_County), in 1882.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-pucabout-8) The creation of schools in the state was urged by [Ellen G. White](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellen_G._White) and other church leaders in an effort to accommodate the Adventist Church's growing membership on the [West Coast](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States) and to train young Adventists for its work. The academy officially opened on April 11 of that year. It was the twelfth institution of higher education founded in California, and is the second founded by the Adventist Church, the first west of the [Mississippi River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mississippi_River).
[Sidney Brownsberger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sidney_Brownsberger) served as its first President. Under his term, the academy focused on both conventional study of standard subjects as well as practical skills, such as [dressmaking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dressmaker), [blacksmithing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blacksmith), [carpentry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carpentry), and [cooking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooking), in line with White's desire for the college.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:8-9)[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-10) The lengthy tenure of William C. Grainger saw the heyday of the Academy's early years, but with the turn of the century, poor financial management led to increasing debt that eventually forced the academy to close in July 1908.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-11)
**Move to Angwin (1909–1921)**
An early image of Pacific Union College's new campus in Angwin
Despite this failure, many church leaders – including White herself – continued to push for expanded Adventist schooling, and efforts were begun in the 1900s to find a new location to rebuild the college. Many sites were scouted out in the [Central Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Valley_(California)) and elsewhere within the state, but none came to the satisfaction of the searchers.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5)
Eventually, in 1909, the [Pacific Union Conference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_Division_of_Seventh-day_Adventists) announced that it had found an opening to purchase the 1,636 acres of the [Angwin Resort](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angwin,_California) on [Howell Mountain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howell_Mountain,_California) in neighboring [Napa County](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napa_County). The property had been found through the church's St. Helena Sanitarium, and White visited the site in September in 1909.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:9-13) Satisfied with the condition of its facilities and living quarters and the ease with which they could be adapted for teaching purposes, its abundant resources in [springs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spring_(hydrology)) and [lumber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lumber), and the healthful living its geography would provide, White approved the location.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:8-9)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:9-13) The property was bought in the same month for $60,000 (roughly $1.9 million in 2022), and opened its doors September 29.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:8-9)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-14) The name of the college was finalized as **Pacific Union College** in 1910 to reflect change in location, even as it had changed names a few times before.
PUC's first president at Angwin was Charles W. Irwin, who served from the opening of the new location until 1921. Irwin's term was marked by [self-sufficiency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-sustainability) as the college adapted to its new location, with both the faculty and student body working to expand the campus through the area's available [natural resources](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resource).[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5)
**Early 20th century (1922–1943)**
In the 1920s and 1930s, PUC expanded its educational programs with the goal of receiving [educational accreditation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Educational_accreditation). Driven by suggestions from the college's [board](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Board_of_directors), PUC required that professors have [postgraduate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Postgraduate_education) degrees to teach, created lower and [upper divisions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Upper_division_college), introduced [major](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academic_major) and [minor degrees](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academic_minor), and necessitated the completion of [senior theses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thesis) for graduation. The college also extended funds to pay for faculty members' graduate studies.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5) With these changes, PUC became the first Adventist college to become accredited when it was awarded accreditation by the [Northwest Association of Secondary and Higher Schools](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Northwest_Association_of_Secondary_and_Higher_Schools) in 1933.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-jaetimeline-15)
This era also saw further expansion of the campus community through the construction of more facilities – including new men's [dormitories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dormitory) and its current [gymnasium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gym) – as well as through the creation of the college's first [student association](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Students%27_union), the Associated Students of Pacific Union College (ASPUC), in January 1935. The college's student newspaper, the _Campus Chronicle_, published its first issue in 1925 after being adapted from the previous _Mountain Echo_, while the _Diogenes Lantern_, PUC's current [yearbook](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yearbook), was first published in 1938.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5)
With the United States' entry into [World War II](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_War_II), over 400 male students and [alumni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alumni) were eventually [drafted](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conscription) into military service.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:4-5)
**Other history (1943–2023)**
In 2006 the faculty, administration, and Board of Trustees underscored PUC's commitment to undergraduate education by making a formal decision to remain a college and not change its name to university, as other small private colleges had done.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-reaccred-16)
In 2006, PUC's Board of Trustees made plans to transform the area into an [ecovillage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecovillage) of several hundred settlements, in partnership with Triad Development, a [Seattle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seattle)-based construction firm.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-17) Although the college downscaled its original plans due to community opposition – primarily by **Save Rural Angwin**, a local [NIMBY](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NIMBY) group – the board voted in October 2010 to sever its contract with Triad and cancel the project.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-18)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:1-19)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-chronicle-20)
Pacific Union College has had a total of [twenty-four presidents](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_Pacific_Union_College). The first eight of these served while the school was still in Healdsburg. In 1983, Malcolm Maxwell became the first alumnus to lead PUC, serving for a record 18 years. Ralph Trecartin, the current president, took office in July 2021 after serving as the associate provost and dean of the College of Professionals for [Andrews University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrews_University).[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-21)
This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding to it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Union_College&action=edit&section=). _(January 2023)_
Academics
Pacific Union College is the only four-year college located in [Napa County, California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napa_County,_California).[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-22) It offers around 60 undergraduate majors in various fields, along with other types of programs. [Health science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Health_science), [business](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Business), and [education](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Education) are the leading fields in which students seek out degrees, and their degree programs are [accredited](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Programmatic_Accreditation) by their [respective accreditation bodies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#accreditation).[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:6-6)
**Curriculum**
Pacific Union College offers 44 [bachelor's degree](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree) programs, 10 [associate degree](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Associate_degree) programs, and three [master's degree](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Master%27s_degree) programs, in addition to minors, credential programs, pre-professional tracks, and an honors program.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:0-23) These are all organized throughout 25 academic departments.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:3-7) The school operates on a quarter-based academic calendar.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:13-24)
Though the range of its offerings is quite broad, PUC's most prominent programs are those in the [health sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Outline_of_health_sciences), which are sought out by a considerable number of those who attend. In the 2020–2021 school year, two-thirds of all undergraduate degrees awarded were in the medical field, and 96% of the associate's degrees awarded were for nursing.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:6-6)
PUC maintains an especially close connection to [Loma Linda University School of Medicine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loma_Linda_University_School_of_Medicine), another Adventist institution, and most of the college's pre-professional programs are meant specifically for admission into Loma Linda.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:3-7) The college has sent a steady stream of students to the university for several decades; visiting from Loma Linda in 1925, [John H. Kellogg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Harvey_Kellogg) noted that PUC was "the college that sends the largest number of medical students from any one place."[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-25) Degrees in business and education follow behind as the second two most sought-after.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:6-6)
Similar to its emphasis on [manual labor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manual_labour) and [physical health](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physical_health) in its Healdsburg days, PUC necessitates that students takes fitness classes as part of its general education requirements.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:3-7) Offerings in the past have included [fencing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fencing), [trikke](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trikke), [pickleball](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pickleball), [swimming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swimming_(sport)), [water aerobics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_aerobics), [polo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polo), [canoeing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canoeing), [skiing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skiing), [snowboarding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Snowboarding), [soccer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soccer), [dance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dance), and [yoga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yoga), though some of these have since been discontinued.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-26)
Similar to other Adventist schools, PUC offers study-abroad programs through **Adventist Colleges Abroad** (**ACA**), primarily in Europe and Latin America.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-27)[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-28) Most of these programs were designed for those seeking degrees from the college's World Languages Department, though non-language majors often study abroad through the ACA as well.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-29)[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-30) PUC also organizes [mission trips](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Short-term_mission) independently of ACA.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-31)[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-32)[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-33)
**Rankings**
At the start of the 2023–2024, the _[U.S. News & World Report](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report)_ ranked Pacific Union College the 13th best regional college in the [Western United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_United_States), the 12th top performer in [social mobility](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_mobility) (a position in shares with the [Fashion Institute of Design & Merchandising](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fashion_Institute_of_Design_%26_Merchandising)), and the second Best Value School.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:13-24) In the same year, the _[New York Times](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_New_York_Times)_ ranked PUC the 8th most economically diverse college in the U.S. [Niche](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Niche_(company)) gives the college an overall B− score, and ranks it the 24th most diverse college in California, with its diversity graded A+. It also ranks PUC as the 14th best nursing college in California.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-34)
In 2024, _[Washington Monthly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Washington_Monthly)_ ranked Pacific Union College 35th among 223 colleges that award almost exclusively bachelor's degrees in the U.S. based on its contribution to the public good, as measured by social mobility, research, and promoting public service. [[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-35)
**Accreditation**
Pacific Union College has been [accredited](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Higher_education_accreditation_in_the_United_States) by the [WASC Senior College and University Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WASC_Senior_College_and_University_Commission) or its predecessor since 1951.[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-36) In February 2020, however, the commission issued a formal Notice of Concern regarding the college's accreditation, citing PUC's dwindling financial resources and dramatic drops in enrollment as areas that needed improvement.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-37) Though in the years following PUC noted moderate increases in enrollment and dismissed a number of its employees in response to WSCUC's recommendations, the commission has not withdrawn its Notice of Concern.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-38)[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-39)[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-40) PUC is also accredited by the Adventist Church's own [Adventist Accrediting Association](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adventist_Accrediting_Association).[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-41)[[a]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-42)
In addition to these two institution-wide accreditations, many of PUC's programs and departments are accredited or approved by their respective programmatic accreditation bodies, including:
- [National Association of Schools of Art and Design](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Association_of_Schools_of_Art_and_Design)[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-43)
- [National Association of Schools of Music](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Association_of_Schools_of_Music)[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-44)
- California Commission on Teacher Credentialing[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:11-45)[[b]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-46)
- [International Assembly for Collegiate Business Education](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Accreditation_Council_for_Business_Education)[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:10-47)
- [Council on Social Work Education](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Council_on_Social_Work_Education)[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-48)
- [Accrediting Commission for Education in Nursing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accrediting_Commission_for_Education_in_Nursing)[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-49)
Campus and facilities
The college is located in [Angwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angwin,_California), on [Howell Mountain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howell_Mountain,_California) overlooking the [Napa Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napa_Valley), 70 miles (110 km) north of [San Francisco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San_Francisco) and 60 miles (85 km) from the Pacific Ocean. The main campus comprises about only 200 acres (0.81 km2) of the college's 1,800 acres (7.3 km2) in property.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:3-7) The remaining 1,600 acres (6.5 km2) is managed as a [conservation easement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conservation_easement).[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:2-50)
Since 2007, [Bon Appétite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bon_App%C3%A9tit_Management_Company) has catered the **PUC Dining Commons**. It serves exclusively vegetarian and vegan menu items sourced from local producers, in accordance with [Adventist health beliefs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church#Health_and_diet) and the company's own catering methods.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:3-7)[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-51) During winter 2011, the Commons saw heavy renovations which redesigned the cafeteria's architectural style and expanded its space.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-52) In 2021, [supply chain shortages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2021%E2%80%932023_global_supply_chain_crisis) caused by [COVID-19](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic) disrupted the kitchen's ability to source local ingredients.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-53)
The college's main library is the **Nelson Memorial Library**, with holdings of around 150,000 books.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:3-7) It also houses the **Pitcairn Islands Study Center**, with a collection of materials about the [Pitcairn Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pitcairn_Islands), as well as literary collections for various figures in [Adventist history](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_the_Seventh-day_Adventist_Church). In 2011, the library was renovated at a cost of over a million dollars.[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-54)

Entrance sign at the front of PUC's campus.**Albion Field Station**
_Main article: [Albion Field Station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albion_Field_Station)_
The college owns as operates the **Albion Field Station**, in [Mendocino County](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mendocino_County), on the [Albion River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albion_River) by the Pacific coast. Though the station was designed for educational purposes, and PUC's [biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biology) department makes use of the river's tide pools and estuaries for course learning, its cabins and other facilities have made it a center of broader student life, and sees visits from other departments.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-55)[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-56)[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-57)[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-58)
This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding to it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Union_College&action=edit&section=). _(January 2023)_
**Angwin–Parrett Field**
_Main article: [Angwin-Parrett Field](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angwin-Parrett_Field)_
Pacific Union College owns and operates Angwin–Parrett Field, a public use airport located on its campus.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-FAA-59) The airport was the landing spot during [George W. Bush](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_W._Bush)'s presidential visit to the Napa Valley in 2006.[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-60) The airport also supports PUC's Bachelor of Science degree in aviation and offers ground schools and flight instruction to the community.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-61)
**PUC Forest**
Much of the school's undeveloped acreage is managed as the **PUC** **Demonstration & Experimental Forest**, though on campus it has sometimes been called the **Back 40**.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:2-50) The forest was part of the [Angwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angwin,_California) property and a site of lumber before it was bought by the college. It spans roughly 1,600 acres (6.5 km2). In addition to providing the resources necessary for the expansion of the campus in its early years, the forest has also served as a site for biological research and preservation, enclosing as it does unique [species](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Species) and [biodiversity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biodiversity).[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:2-50)[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:12-62)
In 2018, PUC partnered with the [Napa County Land Trust](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Napa_County,_California#Agricultural_preservation) to preserve the forest as a [conservation easement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conservation_easement); the easement is currently held by [CalFire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California_Department_of_Forestry_and_Fire_Protection).[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:2-50)[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:12-62) Covering 864 acres (3.50 km2), the easement protects about half of college's total property. It was valued at $7.1 million, much of it due to [vineyard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vineyard) potential.[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:12-62) Attempts in the past have been made to develop or sell the land, however; most notably, the college made plans to transform the area into an [ecovillage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecovillage) in 2006, but it ultimately chose to cancel the project.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:1-19)
Still, with a network of hiking trails spanning 35 miles, the forest sees regular use. In 2019, the college partnered with Napa County's Open Space District to open its trail network to the public.[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-63)[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-64) The forest is also a section along the [Bay Area Ridge Trail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bay_Area_Ridge_Trail), and was linked in the same year.[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-65)[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-66) The [2009 Tour of California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2009_Tour_of_California), an cycling race held within the state, raced through PUC.[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-67)

The PUC Church Sanctuary in October 2022.

**Pacific Union College Church**
_Main article: [Pacific Union College Church](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College_Church)_
Pacific Union College Church is the campus church, built in 1968.[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-PUCCAbout-68) It has around 1,600 members.[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-69) The church's large [pipe organ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College_Church#Rieger_Pipe_Organ) was built by Austrian organ-maker [Rieger Orgelbau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rieger_Orgelbau) and installed in 1981.[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-PUCCrieger-70)[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-71) The church complex also has classrooms for theology classes and houses PUC's Office of Service, Justice, and Missions.
Student life
Pacific Union College's stated focus is on undergraduate education.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-reaccred-16) In the fall of 2021, 829 students were enrolled at PUC, 825 of whom were undergraduate students.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:6-6) The school maintains a student/teacher ratio of roughly 12:1.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-pucabout-8) As a residential college, the vast majority of these students live in one of seven on-campus residence halls.
**Demographics**
Within PUC's student body, the three largest ethnic demographics are [Hispanic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hispanic) (31% in fall 2021), [Asian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asian_people) (23%), and [White](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_people) (21%), while the remaining quarter includes [Black](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/African_Americans) students and others. Female students make up a majority of those on campus (63%), while male students comprise about a third (37%). A large majority of students are from California (78%), with only a fraction enrolling from out of state.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:6-6) PUC was ranked as the most diverse liberal arts college in the [Western United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_United_States) by the _[U.S. News & World Report](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report)_ for the 2022–2023 school year.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-72)
PUC also maintains a [DEI Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diversity,_equity,_and_inclusion) as well as a [Title IX Office](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Title_IX).[[71]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-73) It accepts the WSCUC criteria for diversity: [race](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Race_(classification_of_humans)), [ethnicity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ethnicity), [socioeconomic class](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Socioeconomic_class), [gender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gender), [age](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ageing), [religious belief](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Religious_belief), [sexual orientation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexual_orientation) and [disability](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disability).[[72]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-diversity-74)
**Student association**
The **Pacific Union College [Student Association](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Students%27_union)** (**PUCSA**) was started in 1887, just five years after the college was founded.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)_] It consists of an executive branch and a Student Senate. PUCSA funds publication of the school's student-run newspaper, the _Campus Chronicle,_ and the college yearbook, the _Diogenes Lantern_.
**Student organizations**
There are more than 50 clubs, Honor's Associations and Student Ministries active on campus at Pacific Union College. These include the [Secular Student Alliance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secular_Student_Alliance), Biology Club, Asian Student Association, Pre-Med Club, Korean Adventist Student Association, Dramatic Arts Society, Musical Arts Symposium, Homeless Ministry, [Psi Chi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psi_Chi), [College Democrats](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/College_Democrats) and others. In addition to the _Campus Chronicle_, there are several other student-run publications including a literary periodical, _Quicksilver_.

The logo of Gay and Straight People (GASP) in the early 2020s.**Gay and Straight People**
A [gay-straight alliance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gay-straight_alliance), **Gay and Straight People** (**GASP**), has operated on campus since October 2008. It was first organized by a pre-med student named Jonathan Heldt, who established the alliance when he was a senior in the hopes of creating a [support group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Support_group) for [LGBTQ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LGBT) students and their [allies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Straight_ally).[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:7-75) Originally intending to form an official club, Heldt's proposal was rejected by PUC's Advisory Council.[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-spectrummagazine.org-76)
Students and faculty continue to meet regularly to discuss LGBTQ issues.[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-spectrummagazine.org-76)[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-77) Though the club remains formally unrecognized by the college, the group contributed to the expansion of [all-gender bathrooms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unisex_public_toilet) on campus, and has had presence at the college's student orientation and club fairs.[[76]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:5-78)[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:7-75) In December 2022, the Student Senate passed a bill calling for GASP's recognition as an official club, though it has yet to be addressed by administration.[[76]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:5-78)[[77]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-79)
Athletics
The Pacific Union athletic teams are called the Pioneers. The college is a member of the [National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Association_of_Intercollegiate_Athletics) (NAIA), primarily competing in the [California Pacific Conference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California_Pacific_Conference) (CalPac) since the 1996–97 academic year.
Pacific Union competes in eight intercollegiate sports: Men's sports include basketball, cross country, soccer and volleyball; while women's sports include basketball, cross country, soccer and volleyball.
**Accomplishments**
PUC has been awarded the "California Pacific Conference Team Sportsmanship Award" five times since 2003, most recently for the 2010–2011 school year.[[78]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-80) In fall 2011, the coaches for varsity women's volleyball and men's soccer described it as "rebuilding" time.[[79]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-81) This award signifies the school that displays outstanding sportsmanship and exemplifies the true spirit of the "Champions of Character" program set forth by the NAIA.[[80]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-82)[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-83)
**Intramurals**
PUC maintains an active intramural athletic program under the name RecRadio.org.[[82]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-84)[[83]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-85) The intramural athletic program is the top intramural athletics program in the country according to College Prowler's "Best Intramural Sports" ranking.[[84]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-collegeprowlerintramural-86)
Alumni
_Main article: [List of Pacific Union College alumni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Pacific_Union_College_alumni)_
Pacific Union College has been described as a "training ground for an inordinately large number of outstanding physicians, dentists, nurses, teachers and theologians" who make up its 50,000 alumni.[[85]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-acchist-87) A large majority of students who graduate from the college do so with degrees in the [health sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Outline_of_health_sciences).[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-:6-6) Most of PUC's notable alumni include individuals in [academic administration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academic_administration) or [political offices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Official), though some have been recognized in other fields.[[85]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Union_College#cite_note-acchist-87) Pacific Union College Cecelia Shepard is buried in St. Helena Services are held for Cecelia Shepard at PUC Cecelia Ann Shepard Dean at Pacific Union College Joanne (Sunbather) Linda (Sunbather) Linda L. Jensen (Sunbather) Napa County, CA Angwin, CA California United States of America SF Bay Area St. Helena, CA Pacific Union College Church Pacific Union College

items.title: William Langley Beeman
items.subtitle: Tied to Suspect
items.searchText:
William Langley Beeman Tied to Suspect ## **William Langley Beeman**
## **OBITUARY**
Jan. 7, 1921 – Feb. 1, 2009 Attorney William Langley Beeman died on February 1 at Kaiser Hospital in Vallejo. He was 88 years old.
Bill was born on January 7, 1921, in Fort Worth, Texas. He was the 10th of 10 children of Edwin A. and Lucille E. (Shoemake) Beeman. In his early childhood, the family lived on a farm outside of Golden, Oklahoma. Bill moved to Vallejo the first time with his mother, brother Jack and sister Ruth in October of 1928, residing in Hudson Alley. While attending Lincoln Elementary School and Vallejo Junior High School, he helped to support his family by selling newspapers for five years, seven days a week.
In 1933, Bill moved with his mother and brother Jack to Old Town, in San Diego, where he again sold newspapers for most of his high school years. He graduated from Point Loma High School in 1939 and then attended San Diego State College.
While attending college, he worked as a night watchman for the Bill Gibbs Flying School, being paid with flying lessons. Discovering an enormous love of flying, he quickly obtained his commercial and flight instructors’ licenses. Prior to World War II, he taught flying for the Civilian Pilot Training Program in the Imperial Valley. In June of 1941 he became a flight instructor at Falcon Field in Mesa, Arizona, teaching Royal Air Force cadets skills in primary, basic and advanced flying.
In June of 1942, Bill went to work as a co-pilot for Consairway in San Diego, delivering PBYs and B-24s for the war effort. In 1943, he then became a flight captain for Consairway. Flying under contract for the Army and Navy Transport Commands, Bill’s responsibilities took him through the entire South Pacific, including Australia, New Guinea, Fiji Islands, Biak, Funafuti, and Tarawa. Between flights across the South Pacific, he was a test pilot and delivered planes to the East Coast.
In August of 1945, Bill Beeman married Nancy Jane Chandler from Vacaville, and within a few months he was drafted. He served in Korea with the Army Air Office in Seoul, and was honorably discharged in 1947.
Continuing his flying career, he then worked as an instructor with the Vacaville Dixon Flying Service until he moved to Pacific Overseas Airways in Los Angeles and then Peruvian International Airlines in Lima, Peru. When his flying career ended, Bill had completed over 6,000 hours of flying time.
After he was seriously burned in a fuel explosion and experienced significant legal problems as a result, Bill decided to enter the legal profession. In 1949, he started night school at the McGeorge College of Law in Sacramento. During this time, he worked as a bridge tender and telegraph switch operator for the Southern Pacific Railroad. In the last 15 months of law school, he worked as a U.S. Deputy Clerk for the Federal Court. He graduated with distinction from law school as second in his class, and started his own practice in Vallejo in January of 1954.
During his 50 plus years in the practice of law, Attorney Beeman pursued each of his cases with a fiercely competitive spirit, applying his keen intellect, his gift of storytelling, and his loyalty to the rule of law. In short, he hated to lose. In his trial work, no one ever accused Bill Beeman of a lack of effort. Throughout his law practice, he was genuinely interested in his clients, treating each with respect and sincere friendship.
Bill was devoted to Vallejo and Solano County. He led the efforts to establish the Solano County Legal Aid and Mental Health Programs. He was a lifetime member of the NAACP and served as president of the Solano County Bar Association. He enriched many childrens’ lives as a swimming instructor for the YMCA. Bill was also an energetic supporter of the Democratic Party, being involved in the local Democratic Club and in many local campaigns of state and national candidates.
In 1969, he married Eileen Posedel of Vallejo. He enjoyed golf, fishing, and waterskiing. Throughout his whole life, Bill was busy with his law practice, or community work, or political involvement, or writing, or family É all at the same time.
Bill Beeman is survived by his children, Paul L. Beeman (Donna Kenney), Rockne C. Beeman, Kimberly S. Vaughn (Bill), William L. Beeman II, and Frederick P. Beeman; his grandchildren, Lauren, William, Andrew, Ryan and Sarah; and great-grandchild, Kylie.
Services will be held on Saturday, February 21, at 11 a.m. at First United Methodist Church, 502 Virginia Street, Vallejo.
In lieu of flowers, donations may be made to the American Lung Association. William Langley Beeman William Beeman Sr. accuses brother Jack William Beeman tells Tom Voigt that he's backing off of accusing his brother Vallejo, CA The Internet

items.title: American Cryptogram Association
items.subtitle: Organization
items.searchText: American Cryptogram Association Organization The **American Cryptogram Association** (**ACA**) is an American [non-profit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-profit) organization devoted to the [hobby](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hobby) of [cryptography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptography), with an emphasis on types of codes, ciphers, and [cryptograms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptograms) that can be solved either with pencil and paper, or with computers, but not computer-only systems. items.title: Barbara Lowe
items.subtitle: Medical
items.searchText:
Barbara Lowe Medical 321 Cypress Street, Vallejo CA

_Solano police report_
12:05AM 12/21/68 Det/Sgt. Lundblad arrived at the scene. Sgt. Lundblad requested RO's go to Vallejo Hospital to try to get a statement from the boy (FARADAY) 12:23AM 987 Vallejo General Hospital. Contacted nurse, Mrs. Barbara Lowe, 321 Cypress Street. RO requested permission to see FARADAY. Mrs. Lowe stated FARADAY was dead on arrival at the hospital. He was pronounced as dead at 12:05AM by Dr. Siebert. RO checked FARADAY. He had been shot in the upper portion of the left ear by a small caliber bullet which penetrated the ear and head. There was what appeared to be powder burns on the left ear where the bullet had entered. There was a large lump on his right cheek and his hair on the left side of his head was matted with blood. There was also blood on his hands and the sleeves of his shirt. He was wearing a light blue long sleeve shirt, brown corduroy pants-levi type, black socks, tan leather shoes-rough texture. He had a Timex wrist watch, chrome case and band, on his left wrist, 85¢ (3-quarters and one dime) in his left front pants pocket, a black comb in his left hip pocket, a white handkerchief and small bottle of breath drops (Binaca) in his right front pants pocket. Barbara Lowe David Faraday pronounced dead Vallejo General Hospital Vallejo, CA Solano County, CA California SF Bay Area United States of America Murders at Lake Herman Road

items.title: James A. Owen
items.subtitle: Witness
items.searchText: James A. Owen Witness CMSGT JAMES OWEN U.S. Air Force, Retired James Alford Owen was born June 14, 1930 in Kalamazoo Mich. He married the love of his life, Verna Larsen, June 10, 1950. During the next 62 years, they raised three children, two sons Kenneth and Timothy, and a daughter, Laura. Jim entered into the U.S. Army at 17, at the end of World War II, and became a member of the U.S. Air Force when it first separated from the Army Air Corp. He served proudly for 20 years, retiring from the Air Force with many distinguishing honors, not the least becoming one of the original Chief Master Sergeants after the rank of E-9 was created. Shortly after retiring from his military service, he worked at Exxon Oil Refinery in Benicia, Calif. as a risk manager. Jim passed quietly at home Thursday morning, Aug. 16, 2012, after a return of the cancer. In addition to his three children, Jim is survived by nine grandchildren; and 14 great- grandchildren. Memorial services will be at 1 p.m., Wednesday, Aug. 22, at Palm Mortuary, 7600 S. Eastern Ave., Las Vegas, NV 89123. James A. Owen James Owen passes by crime scene, sees two cars Humble Oil / Exxon / Valero Refinery Benicia, CA Solano County, CA California SF Bay Area United States of America Lake Herman Road Zodiac Site Lake Herman Road

items.title: Russell T. Butterbach
items.subtitle: Law Enforcement
items.searchText:
Russell T. Butterbach Law Enforcement Jan. 29, 1920 – May 26, 2008 Russell Butterbach, 88, passed away on Monday after a short battle with leukemia. He was born in Joliet, IL and was a WW II veteran who enlisted in the Army Air Corps on Dec. 8, 1941. He served in North Africa and Italy until the end of the war.
Russell was a man with an enthusiastic and positive view of life that was contagious to those around him. He was a dedicated police officer and retired from the Solano County Sheriff’s Office in 1976 after 16 years of duty. He enjoyed a long and happy retirement that included golfing, singing, playing the drums and piano, flying his son-in-law’s airplane and making friends wherever he went. Most recently he appeared as a technical consultant on the Director’s Cut of the Zodiac movie discussing his involvement in the case.
He is survived by his wife of 65 years, Hope Butterbach; daughters Sharon Downey, Karen Eckerson and Debra McClanahan; son-in-law, Dean Eckerson; grandchildren Tracy Porten, Russell Miller, Mark Downey and Andrew Dussell; and great-grandchildren Theodore and Contessa Porten, Ethan Dussell, and Riley Miller.
A private memorial is planned for family and close friends. In lieu of flowers, contributions to Kaiser Hospice, 975 Sereno Drive, Vallejo are appreciated. Russell T. Butterbach Lake Herman Road Double Murder Solano County Sheriff's Office California Solano County, CA Vallejo, CA SF Bay Area United States of America Lake Herman Road Zodiac Site Murders at Lake Herman Road
